--- Page 1 ---
SMGI: A Structural Theory of General Artificial Intelligence
Preprint
Aomar OSMANI
INSA-Rouen, France
aomar.osmani@insa-rouen.fr
March 2026
This manuscript is a preprint
Abstract
We introduce SMGI, a structural theory of general artificial intelligence, and recast the
foundational problem of learning from the optimization of hypotheses within fixed environments
to the controlled evolution of the learning interface itself. We formalize the Structural Model
of General Intelligence (SMGI) via a typed meta-model θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) that treats
representational maps, hypothesis spaces, structural priors, multi-regime evaluators, and memory
operators as explicitly typed, dynamic components. By enforcing a strict mathematical separation
between this structural ontology (θ) and its induced behavioral semantics (Tθ), we define general
artificial intelligence as a class of admissible coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ) satisfying four obligations:
structural closure under typed transformations, dynamical stability under certified evolution,
bounded statistical capacity, and evaluative invariance across regime shifts. We prove a structural
generalization bound that links sequential PAC-Bayes analysis and Lyapunov stability, providing
sufficient conditions for capacity control and bounded drift under admissible task transformations.
Furthermore, we establish a strict structural inclusion theorem demonstrating that classical
empirical risk minimization, reinforcement learning, program-prior models (Solomonoff-style),
and modern frontier agentic pipelines operate as structurally restricted instances of SMGI.
Grounded in epistemological constraints that separate descriptive prediction from normative
evaluation, this framework proposes a mathematically explicit extension of statistical learning
theory where evaluation itself becomes an object of certified evolution. Finally, we propose an
empirical protocol for measuring structural growth and memory-governed stability in long-horizon,
nonstationary regimes.
1
Introduction
Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is a foundational scientific objective: to characterize learning-
and-decision systems that remain coherent and effective across heterogeneous task regimes, evolving
interfaces, and shifting evaluative conditions. Contemporary systems have demonstrated striking
advances in language modeling, multimodal integration, tool-augmented reasoning, and long-horizon
planning OpenAI [2023a], Anthropic [2024], Google DeepMind [2024], Team [2024a,d], Brown et al.
[2020]. Yet a central theoretical question remains unresolved:
What structural principle distinguishes scalable task generalization from genuinely general
intelligence?
1
arXiv:2603.07896v1  [cs.AI]  9 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
Diagnosis:
Local guarantees versus structural transformations.
Existing theoretical
frameworks provide powerful local guarantees. Statistical learning theory controls generalization
within fixed hypothesis classes Vapnik [1998a], Valiant [1984]; PAC-Bayes and MDL/SRM refine
capacity control under fixed representations and losses McAllester [1999a], Catoni [2007], Gr"unwald
[2007]; domain adaptation quantifies transfer under a fixed input–output interface Ben-David et al.
[2010], Arjovsky et al. [2019]; and reinforcement learning formalizes sequential decision-making
under fixed state/action semantics Mnih et al. [2015], Schulman et al. [2017]. However, modern
agentic deployments increasingly operate under structural transformations: new tools and interfaces,
shifting objectives, multi-regime evaluation, evolving memory and retrieval policies, and adversarially
perturbed contexts Yao et al. [2023], Park et al. [2023], Greshake et al. [2023]. In such settings, the
learning problem is to generalize across tasks while also remaining controllable under admissible
changes of the interface itself.
The fragmentation of current solutions: partial internal fixes and external wrappers.
To address these agentic regime shifts, advanced RL and continual-learning lines have introduced
internal but partial mechanisms: hierarchical RL and option invention aim to restructure policies
and reuse temporally extended skills [Nayyar and Srivastava, 2024, Shek and Tokekar, 2025a,b], while
continual learning proposes global alignment and structured sparse memory updates to mitigate
catastrophic forgetting [Bai et al., 2024b,a, Lin et al., 2025]. In parallel, the dominant industry
response to safety has been to attach external governance components around otherwise fixed
learners. This includes constitutional or principle-based post-training [Bai et al., 2022a,b], guardrail
and monitoring layers [Dong et al., 2024b,a], and runtime formal verification or shielding [Corsi and
et al., 2024]. These approaches are valuable, but they typically treat norms, verifiers, evaluators,
memory stability, and admissible transformations as fragmented modules or exogenous wrappers
rather than as first-class objects in the learning meta-model.
Why safety and security force a structural theory.
This fragmentation is increasingly
problematic as safety becomes a complex evaluation-and-assurance problem. System cards and
preparedness frameworks now explicitly separate multi-domain threat regimes [OpenAI, 2023b,
2024a, Phuong et al., 2024, OpenAI, 2024b, Autio et al., 2024]. Furthermore, tool-integrated agents
expose interface-level vulnerabilities—such as indirect prompt injection and plan backdoors—that
are now systematically benchmarked [Greshake et al., 2023, Zhan et al., 2024, Ramakrishna et al.,
2024, Zhang et al., 2024a, Yi et al., 2023, Zhang et al., 2024b]. Crucially, memory itself has become
an attack surface (e.g., memory and RAG poisoning), making invariant-preserving updates central
to controllability [Chen et al., 2024].
Why agentic evaluation makes internalization unavoidable.
Consequently, the field’s
evaluation paradigm is radically shifting from static end-task scores to the correctness of stateful multi-
step tool-use, memory-dependent decisions, and long-horizon workflows (e.g., BFCL, TRAJECT-
Bench, AgentBench) [Patil et al., 2025, He et al., 2025, Liu et al., 2024], prompting unified taxonomies
that separate policy models from evaluators and dynamic components [Li, 2025].
Together, these developments motivate the view that norms, evaluators, and admissible transfor-
mations should not be treated solely as exogenous evaluation infrastructure. We therefore model
them as first-class objects within the learning meta-structure.
Thesis: structural generalization and control-by-structure.
We advance the thesis that
general artificial intelligence cannot be reduced to broader task coverage under a fixed evaluative
2


--- Page 3 ---
regime. Instead it requires structural generalization: the capacity to represent, compare, revise, and
preserve parts of the learning interface itself under task transformations. This implies control-by-
structure: evolution must be restricted to certified admissible transformations, explicitly separating
(i) what may change (typed transformations and admissible updates), (ii) what must remain invariant
(identity/personality invariants and protected evaluators), and (iii) what must be verified at each
step (normative constraints and monitors). The goal is not a new architecture, but a formal object
that makes structural change auditable and analyzable.
Epistemological necessity: from external metrics to structural objects.
This internaliza-
tion of governance is not merely an engineering convenience; it is required for stating evaluation and
admissibility conditions within the learning object. Following the classical distinction between facts
and values Hume [1739], evaluation cannot be purely derived from descriptive prediction. The loss or
utility functional encodes a normative semantics that fixes what counts as an error or an acceptable
trade-off. Similarly, in a Kantian spirit, the representational interface r is constitutive: it determines
what distinctions are learnable and comparable, thereby inducing the system’s active ontology Kant
[1998]. If the evaluation regime or the representation changes, the very definition of the task changes.
This dictates that evaluation and representation must be treated as first-class internal components
of the learning object, a foundational argument we fully expand upon in Section 2.
The structural meta-model and the coupled view (θ, Tθ).
To capture representation,
evaluation, memory, and admissible evolution within a single formal object, we introduce a structured
learning meta-model
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M),
Crucially, SMGI is not a property of θ alone but of the coupled object (θ, Tθ), where Tθ denotes
the induced behavioral semantics (the dynamical evolution under learning, memory, and interaction).
This separation between structural ontology and behavioral semantics is what makes stability,
invariants, and evaluative integrity expressible as properties of certified evolution rather than
informal desiderata. Formal typing and notation are given in Sec. 3.
Structural definition of AGI: admissibility obligations.
In this paper, AGI is not defined
by benchmark performance, human equivalence, or parameter scale. We define AGI structurally as
the class of admissible pairs (θ, Tθ) satisfying four obligations:
(i) Closure: the system remains closed under a typed class of admissible task/interface transfor-
mations;
(ii) Stability: certified sequential adaptation preserves dynamical stability under regime switching
and memory interaction;
(iii) Capacity: statistical capacity remains bounded along admissible evolutions (including
implicit/trajectory-dependent regularization effects);
(iv) Evaluative invariance: evaluators and norms remain invariant across regime shifts unless
updated via protected, certified meta-transformations.
What is (and is not) claimed here.
We use the term AGI in a strictly formal sense: not as
a claim about any existing system, but as a structural admissibility concept for evolving learning
systems under typed interface transformations. The present article develops this structural theory
at the level of the meta-model, its induced semantics, and its admissibility obligations.
3


--- Page 4 ---
Minimal checkability (pointer).
In the formal development (Sec. 4), we make these obligations
checkable by requiring explicit witnesses: a declared typed transformation class T , a stability
certificate (Lyapunov-style or equivalent) on the induced dynamics, a capacity/complexity functional
controlling the effective hypothesis/update interface, and a protected invariant evaluative core Φ
together with an admissibility test for evaluator updates.
Why scale is not sufficient (as an instance).
Scaling laws Kaplan et al. [2020], Hoffmann
et al. [2022] and modern analyses of overparameterized learning (e.g., NTK limits and implicit
regularization) Jacot et al. [2018], Arora et al. [2019] help explain generalization under fixed regimes,
and phenomena such as grokking emphasize the role of learning dynamics Power et al. [2022], Wang
et al. [2024]. SMGI complements these insights by lifting capacity control from parameter-space
regularization to structural capacity control over admissible transformations of (r, H, Π, L, E, M),
which becomes essential under multi-regime evaluation and interface transformations.
Contributions.
Our contributions are:
1. A minimal structural meta-model for AGI. We formalize the SMGI object θ and its
induced semantics Tθ, clarifying the ontology/semantics split required for structural reasoning.
2. A structural definition of AGI via obligations. We define closure, stability, capacity, and
evaluative invariance as admissibility obligations for (θ, Tθ), making verification meaningful
only relative to explicit typed transformations and protected evaluators.
3. Unified capacity analysis under structural evolution. We connect SRM/MDL, PAC-
Bayes, and stability-based viewpoints to structural capacity control under admissible updates,
including modern implicit-regularization phenomena as instances Gr"unwald [2007], McAllester
[1999a], Catoni [2007], Arora et al. [2019].
4. Strict inclusion and positioning. We show how classical SLT, RL, universal induction, and
frontier LLM pipelines arise as structurally restricted instances of the proposed meta-framework,
clarifying what changes when the interface and evaluator are no longer fixed.
Organization.
Sec. 2 develops the philosophical and scientific foundations. Sec. 3 introduces
formal setup, typing, and notation. Secs. 4–6.3 develop the formal meta-structure, structural
guarantees, and capacity analysis. Sec. 7 positions SMGI within existing theoretical frameworks.
Sec. 7.3 compares SMGI to frontier systems (2023–2026), followed by discussion and conclusions.
2
Philosophical and Scientific Foundations
The formal framework developed in this article, which we call the Structural Model of General
Intelligence (SMGI), is not proposed as a purely technical aggregation. It is motivated by a deeper
claim from the history and philosophy of science: what a system can learn is inseparable from how
it is evaluated, and evaluation is itself a structural commitment that must be formalized. To justify
the internalization of the loss ℓand the representation r into the meta-model θ, we must address
the fundamental epistemological limits of classical learning.
Throughout the article, the term SMGI will denote the class of admissible coupled objects (θ, Tθ)
satisfying structural closure under admissible task transformations, dynamical stability, bounded
capacity, and evaluative invariance. When no ambiguity arises, we refer to the corresponding
meta-model θ alone by abuse of notation.
4


--- Page 5 ---
2.1
Facts, Values, and the Structure of Evaluation
The irreducibility of normative evaluation.
A foundational difficulty in defining general
artificial intelligence is that accurate prediction and justified evaluation are logically distinct. Since
Hume’s articulation of the is/ought gap, it has been canonically recognized that no set of purely
descriptive propositions entails a normative conclusion without an additional normative premise
[Hume, 1739]. Consequently, a system may predict environmental dynamics correctly while remaining
entirely unjustified with respect to the criteria used to judge its outputs.
Poincaré restated the same logical constraint in La morale et la science: if the premises of a
reasoning are purely indicative, the conclusion remains indicative; no imperative conclusion can be
obtained from descriptive premises alone [Poincaré, 1920]. Scientific appraisal therefore presupposes
epistemic values such as simplicity, unity, and fecundity that are not derivable from data alone. A
complementary meta-ethical clarification is provided by Moore’s open-question argument, which
makes precise why evaluative predicates such as “good”, “correct”, or “safe” are not reducible
by definition to purely descriptive predicates [Moore, 1903]. Together, these arguments support
a structural separation between descriptive regularities, evaluative mechanisms, and normative
admissibility conditions.
In SMGI, this separation is made explicit at the level of the typed meta-model
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M).
The tuple (r, H, Π) governs the descriptive side of learning: representation, admissible hypothesis
classes, and inductive bias determine which regularities can be expressed and generalized. By
contrast, evaluation is implemented through a family of evaluative functionals
L = {ℓk}K
k=1,
and the active regime-local evaluator takes the form
ℓt(·) =
K
X
k=1
λt,k ℓk(·),
with weights controlled by context and memory. Normativity is not identified with the evaluator
itself: it appears as a system of admissibility constraints, denoted here by Φ, that governs which
evaluators, or evaluator updates, are allowed to count as structurally coherent. In this sense, SMGI
distinguishes three levels that are often conflated: value-laden evaluative dimensions encoded by
the basis family {ℓk}K
k=1, normative constraints encoded by Φ, and evaluation as the operational
mechanism ℓt that ranks hypotheses, actions, or trajectories in a given regime.
This distinction matters mathematically. Even when an evaluator is estimated from data, it
defines a selection functional over hypotheses,
h 7→Ez∼D
ℓt(h, z)
,
and therefore fixes what counts as an error, a violation, or an acceptable trade-off. The resulting
ordering is not derivable from descriptive prediction alone. Following Canguilhem, one may say
that normativity is not an external ornament added after the fact, but part of the very organization
through which a system distinguishes viable from non-viable trajectories [Canguilhem, 1966]. SMGI
operationalizes this point by internalizing evaluative structure as part of the learning ontology rather
than leaving it implicit in the training procedure.
5


--- Page 6 ---
Entanglement and the necessity of value learning.
At the same time, the philosophy of
science emphasizes that facts and values are deeply entangled in scientific practice; this does
not eliminate normativity but reinforces the need to surface evaluative commitments explicitly
rather than leave them implicit [Putnam, 2002]. More generally, normative systems are typically
justified by coherence between principles and judgments under continuous revision—a “reflective
equilibrium”—which motivates the explicit modeling of evaluator update rules rather than treating
them as permanently fixed [Rawls, 1971]. In SMGI, such updates are represented as typed meta-
transformations acting on ℓt (or regime weights) and are constrained by certification and invariance
requirements (see Sec. 2.2).
At a formal level, these normative commitments are modeled via utility and loss functionals
that induce preference orderings over outcomes and policies [von Neumann and Morgenstern, 1944,
Savage, 1954]. In modern agentic settings, however, the relevant objective is rarely fully known a
priori; it is often only partially specified and must be inferred or refined from interaction. This
reality drives contemporary value learning, cooperative inverse reinforcement learning (CIRL), and
preference-based alignment methods [Hadfield-Menell et al., 2016b, Christiano et al., 2017, Russell,
2019]. Canguilhem’s analysis of normativity sharpens the same point from another direction: norms
are not merely external labels attached after the fact, but organizing conditions internal to a
mode of functioning [Canguilhem, 1966]. In SMGI, this is precisely why evaluative admissibility is
represented by explicit constraint systems such as Φ and KΦ, rather than by an undifferentiated
scalar objective.
2.2
Reflective Equilibrium as Certified Updates of the Evaluator
To separate descriptive adaptation from normative consistency, SMGI does not treat the evaluator as
an unconstrained objective updated by naive optimization. Following the Rawlsian idea of reflective
equilibrium [Rawls, 1971], we model evaluator change as a certified reconciliation between empirical
feedback and identity-preserving invariants.
A. Invariants and admissibility.
Let Φ denote a fixed set of identity-preserving invariants
specified at t = 0 (e.g., hard safety constraints, logical consistency requirements, or core normative
commitments). In SMGI, evaluator change is admissible only through updates that preserve Φ on a
certified test class (or audited environments), rather than universally over H.
B. Certified evaluator update operator.
Let ℓt denote the current evaluator (possibly multi-
regime, e.g., ℓt = PK
k=1 λt,k ℓk) and let z1:t and mt denote the data stream and persistent memory.
A candidate update ˜ℓt+1 = Γ(ℓt, z1:t, mt) (e.g., Bayesian update, preference refinement, or meta-
learning) is accepted only if it satisfies the invariants on an audited set Eaudit (or a certified hypothesis
subset Hcert). We assume ℓranges over a parametrized evaluator class L (or, in the multi-regime
case, over regime weights λt in a fixed basis {ℓk}K
k=1). We write this as a certified update operator:
ℓt+1 = CertUpdateΦ(ℓt, z1:t, mt) ≜arg min
ℓ∈KΦ D(ℓ, ˜ℓt+1),
KΦ =
n
ℓ: Φ holds on Eaudit
as certified by monitors/verifiers over audited trajectories
o
,
(1)
where D is a chosen divergence on evaluators (e.g., KL between induced preference models, or a norm
in a parametrized evaluator class), and KΦ is the admissible set enforced by certificates/monitors.
6


--- Page 7 ---
C. Implications for SMGI obligations.
This construction makes evaluator change an explicit
part of certified structural evolution.
Formally, evaluator updates are elements of the typed
transformation class T acting on the ℓ-component of θ: (i) it prevents normative drift by restricting
evaluator updates to KΦ (evaluative invariance), (ii) it makes closure and stability statements
meaningful because admissible transformations include certified evaluator updates, and (iii) it allows
capacity and drift guarantees to be stated on the coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ) under certified evolution
rather than under an exogenous pipeline.
Structural Risk and Capacity.
Vapnik’s structural risk minimization formalizes generalization
by coupling empirical fit and capacity control within a hierarchical organization of hypothesis classes
[Vapnik, 1998a]. This provides a principled foundation for treating capacity constraints as part of
the system’s admissible structure, rather than as an external hyperparameter choice. In this sense,
SRM already contains a proto-structural insight: generalization is not merely a property of data
fitting, but of the hierarchical organization of admissible hypothesis spaces.
Simplicity Priors and Universal Prediction.
A complementary foundational tradition connects
evaluation to structural regularization via simplicity principles. Solomonoff induction and AIXI
encode a prior over programs weighted by description length [Solomonoff, 1964, Hutter, 2005], while
Kolmogorov complexity and MDL formalize simplicity as compressibility and provide operational
regularizers [Li and Vitányi, 2008, Gr"unwald, 2007, Rissanen, 1978]. These results motivate treating
the prior Π not as a heuristic add-on but as a structural component of the meta-model.
Synthesis: The justification of the SMGI Meta-Model.
Under regime shifts, tool augmenta-
tions, and self-modification, an intelligent system faces a meta-problem: it must maintain coherence
between what is represented, how outcomes are evaluated, which priors structure induction, and
which transformations remain admissible without destroying structural identity. Because objectives
and environments are dynamic, this coherence must be understood as a time-indexed process whose
evaluative component is expressed by ℓt and whose admissibility is certified on the coupled dynamics
(θ, Tθ). This is the exact justification for SMGI’s central move: representation, hypothesis space,
prior, evaluator family, environment family, and memory operator are treated as first-class structural
components of the typed meta-model
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M).
This move is philosophically motivated and mathematically necessary. Kantian considerations
explain why representation cannot be external to a theory of intelligence; Humean and Poincaréan
considerations explain why normativity cannot be recovered from descriptive success alone; and
Quine’s underdetermination thesis explains why empirical adequacy does not uniquely fix a single
inductive organization [Kant, 1998, Hume, 1739, Poincaré, 1920, Quine, 1960]. The role of Π is
therefore not heuristic decoration: it formalizes the structural commitments through which a system
privileges some admissible generalizations over others.
Accordingly, SMGI should be read not as a behavioral slogan but as a structural ontology for
admissible general intelligence. The coupled object (θ, Tθ) separates the learning system’s internal
organization from its induced behavioral semantics, making it possible to state closure, stability,
bounded-capacity, and evaluative-invariance obligations at the correct level. In this sense, values,
inductive bias, representation, and persistence are no longer external vulnerabilities of a training
pipeline, but explicit structural components whose joint evolution can be certified.
7


--- Page 8 ---
2.3
Unification as a Scientific Virtue and a Design Constraint
A second foundational motivation is unification.
Newtonian science is a paradigm case: the
unification of heterogeneous phenomena under a single mathematical structure is not merely
aesthetic but constitutive of explanatory power. Modern scholarship clarifies that unification in
Newton is inseparable from methodological commitments about mathematization, idealization, and
the normative role of principles [Ducheyne, 2012]. In our setting, the analogue is that a general
theory of intelligence should not only fit many tasks, but specify the minimal structural objects and
operators under which heterogeneous learning regimes can be represented within a single formal
system. Formally, unification can be understood as the identification of invariant structures under
transformation, specifying state spaces and operators whose stability is preserved under admissible
task transformations.
2.4
Paradigms, Evaluation Criteria, and Theory-Relative Invariants
Kuhn’s analysis of scientific change stresses that what counts as evidence, adequacy, and progress
depends on theory-internal standards that evolve with paradigms [Kuhn, 1962]. This reinforces the
need to treat evaluation as a structured component of a system, not as a fixed external metric. As
established in Section 2.1 via Putnam’s entanglement thesis [Putnam, 2002], this implies that for
learning systems, cross-domain intelligence requires not only updating hypotheses within a fixed
evaluative frame, but maintaining invariants under transformations of tasks and evaluation regimes.
This is precisely the motivating gap for the present work: classical theories formalize capacity
control, sequential decision-making, or universal prediction, but do not simultaneously formalize (i)
a closure notion under task transformation, (ii) stability of a persistent system under sequential
adaptation, and (iii) invariance constraints defining coherent identity across regimes. The notion of
identity here is structural rather than psychological: it refers to the preservation of boundedness,
coherence, and well-defined update rules across transformations of tasks, data distributions, or
evaluative criteria.
2.5
Representation, Direction, and Plurality of Regimes
In the Kantian tradition, cognition presupposes organizing principles that condition the possibility
of experience itself [Kant, 1998]. In SMGI, this point is formalized by treating the representational
map as a constitutive component of the meta-model rather than as a neutral preprocessing device.
Representation is not a passive encoding of a pre-given world: it determines which distinctions
are available to the system and therefore which hypothesis classes are effectively accessible. Put
differently, the representational component r helps determine the induced learnable structure, and
changes in r alter the ontology on which learning and evaluation operate.
Cassirer sharpens this point by emphasizing that cognition proceeds through symbolic forms
rather than through immediate access to raw reality [Cassirer, 1953]. Sellars, in turn, rejects the
myth of a purely given layer of experience that would stand prior to conceptual articulation [Sellars,
1956]. Their common lesson is directly relevant here: neither representation nor evaluation should
be treated as transparent interfaces to an already structured world. In SMGI, they are explicit
structural commitments. The representational component is therefore not merely an encoder but
part of the system’s active ontology. Formally, the typed meta-model
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M)
locates r as a structural primitive, so that r helps determine the effective hypothesis class H(θ) and,
through it, the space of learnable distinctions available to the system.
8


--- Page 9 ---
Schopenhauer’s distinction between world as representation and world as will [Schopenhauer,
1819] highlights a second separation between what is represented and what is directionally pursued.
We capture this distinction by introducing a local structural state
ct = (rt, wt, mt),
rt : Z →H,
wt ∈W,
where mt is persistent memory. Here rt specifies the representational interface, while wt param-
eterizes directional organization: regime activation weights, preference orientation, or constraint
multipliers determining which evaluative dimensions are currently emphasized. This decomposition
is not auxiliary notation but a local dynamic reading of the SMGI ontology: rt instantiates the
representational component, wt parameterizes directional or evaluative orientation, and mt carries
persistence through memory. Peirce’s logic of inquiry provides a further bridge here, since abduction,
revision, and interpretive mediation all presuppose that representation and hypothesis formation
are structurally organized rather than mechanically extracted from data [Peirce, 1931].
Moving from representation to direction, Nietzsche and Freud provide a useful structural analogy
for agency organized by competing evaluative forces rather than by a single homogeneous objective
[Nietzsche, 1887, Freud, 1923]. In SMGI, this motivates extending a single evaluator to a family of
regime-dependent evaluators:
ℓ(·) ⇝{ℓk(·)}K
k=1,
ℓt(·) =
K
X
k=1
λt,k ℓk(·),
(2)
with context-dependent weights λt,k controlled by wt and memory. The point is not that psychoana-
lytic or genealogical theories literally define the update law, but that they correctly foreground a
structural fact: agency may remain coherent while being governed by plural, sometimes competing
evaluative regimes. The philosophical plurality of drives, values, or competing orientations is thus
translated into a typed evaluator family L = {ℓk}K
k=1 together with context- and memory-dependent
activation weights.
Spinoza’s doctrine of conatus adds the complementary requirement of persistence through change:
an intelligent system should not merely vary its active evaluators across regimes, but preserve identity-
relevant invariants through those variations [Spinoza, 1996]. In SMGI, this persistence is expressed
through the admissibility of coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ) under stability and invariance constraints.
Simondon’s analysis of individuation clarifies that such persistence should be understood not as
immobility but as coherent transformation under structured change [Simondon, 2005]. Together,
these perspectives support the central claim of SMGI: regime shifts should be modeled as admissible
structural transformations preserving coherence, rather than as unconstrained jumps between
unrelated optimization problems.
Salience as Structural Weighting.
To separate regime weights from policy notation, we denote
input salience by an operator σt : Z →R+ and write ℓk(h, z) 7→σt(z) ℓk(h, z) when needed. This
formalizes the idea (classically associated with motivation and “passions” in the philosophy of
agency) that evaluation is not only plural but also selectively amplified or suppressed depending
on context. The triple (rt, wt, mt) therefore anticipates a more general structural decomposition in
which representation, evaluation, and persistence are treated as interacting subsystems.
2.6
Engineering Meta-Models and the Necessity of a Meta-Level
The motivation for an explicit meta-structure is not unique to learning theory. In software engineering,
the Meta-Object Facility (MOF) provides a meta-metamodel specifying how modeling languages
9


--- Page 10 ---
themselves are defined [Object Management Group, 2019]. UML, in turn, is a structured meta-
model describing classes, relations, and behavioral dynamics [Object Management Group, 2017].
Correctness and consistency are enforced at the meta-level: constraints are defined over families of
models, not only over individual instances.
Object-oriented languages further illustrate this structural idea: inheritance and subsumption
are not mere implementation tools; they impose formal constraints ensuring that transformations
between objects preserve type consistency and structural invariants [Meyer, 1988, Booch, 1994].
When a domain involves transformations between heterogeneous structures, stability and coherence
require explicit meta-level constraints. SMGI plays an analogous formal role: it defines the space of
admissible learning systems and constrains their evolution under task transformation operators.
Optional Extensions: Meta-Normativity and Ontology.
We note two optional second-order
extensions that fit the same structural discipline but are not required for the main line of the theory.
Two further structural notions can be expressed within the same spirit, while remaining optional in
the main line of the theory. Meta-normativity treats evaluative regimes themselves as objects of
second-order appraisal, via a functional N : K →R measuring coherence, legitimacy, or stability
of regime families. Structural ontology can be summarized by observing that a system induces an
implicit ontology through its representational range: Ω(θ) = Range(r). Different θ therefore induce
different ontological commitments without fixing ontology a priori.
Dimensional Incompleteness and Motivation.
The preceding considerations converge on a
single structural claim: learning is not merely adaptation within a fixed evaluative space; it is the
structured transformation of the evaluative space itself. This motivates the construction of a formal
meta-structure where these constraints are explicit at the level of mathematical definition.
3
Formal Setup, Typing, and Notation
To eliminate ambiguity and keep the formal development closed under composition, we enforce a
typed notation that distinguishes (i) global carriers, (ii) instantiations induced by a meta-structure
θ, and (iii) time-indexed states produced by the induced learning dynamics.
Global objects (problem-independent).
We write X, Y for observation and target spaces, E
for a global carrier of admissible environments (or task-generating processes), and H for a global
hypothesis carrier. These symbols denote carriers rather than a particular learner.
Objects instantiated by θ.
Whenever an object depends on the choice of meta-structure, we
use the unique convention (·)(θ):
H(θ) ⊆H,
P(θ) (policy/parameter space),
M(θ) (memory state space),
E(θ) ⊆E (environment family
Thus H(θ) denotes the effective hypothesis class admitted by θ (e.g., via representational constraints
r and regularization Π), and similarly for P(θ), M(θ), and E(θ).
Priors vs. policies.
We reserve Π for a prior (or structural regularizer) over hypotheses or
programs, used in SRM/PAC-Bayes/MDL-style control. By contrast, π denotes a policy (or decision
rule) belonging to P(θ). In particular:
Π ̸= π,
π ∈P(θ),
Π acts on H(θ) or program space.
10


--- Page 11 ---
Evaluators: active loss vs. regime family.
We reserve L = {ℓk}K
k=1 for the family of regime
evaluators (multi-regime structure) that is part of the structural meta-model. The symbol ℓt denotes
the active evaluator at time t (possibly a selected element of L, or a certified mixture such as
ℓt = PK
k=1 λt,k ℓk). When we write ℓwithout a time index, it denotes a generic element of L (or the
induced evaluator semantics under a fixed regime) and never the whole family.
Time-indexed states.
When the induced dynamics Tθ is considered, we index states in time:
rt ∈R(θ),
πt ∈P(θ),
mt ∈M(θ),
and we write θ only for the (comparatively) static meta-structure. This separation is used throughout
to avoid conflating ontological structure (θ) with behavioral semantics (Tθ).
3.1
The SMGI Meta-Model
The Meta-Model.
To keep notation lightweight in the introduction and foundations, we write
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) for the structural carriers. In the formal development, we distinguish carriers
from their effective instances: a well-typed θ induces (or selects) an effective hypothesis class
H(θ) ⊆H and an effective environment family E(θ) ⊆E (and similarly for memory/state spaces).
To formalize this idea, we introduce a structured learning meta-model
θ = (r, H(θ), Π, L, E(θ), M(θ)).
where:
• r denotes representation operators,
• H(θ) the hypothesis space (effective class admitted by θ),
Evaluator family vs. active evaluator (notation contract).
Throughout the paper,
the evaluator component of the meta-model is a family L = {ℓk}K
k=1 of regime evaluators. The
active evaluator at time t is denoted ℓt ∈L (or, when using convex mixing, ℓt = PK
k=1 λt,kℓk
with λt,· ∈∆K). The single-regime special case is represented as L = {ℓ}, but the signature of
θ remains θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) throughout.
• Π denotes prior or structural regularization,
• ℓevaluative structures (possibly multi-regime L),
• E(θ) environment classes,
• M(θ) persistent structured memory.
Unlike classical learning theory, which optimizes h ∈H under fixed (r, Π, ℓ), we study systems
that operate over θ itself. The object θ defines a coupled dynamical system whose admissible
evolutions are constrained by stability, capacity, and invariance conditions.
Classical paradigms — empirical risk minimization, PAC-Bayes learning, reinforcement learning,
meta-learning, and transformer-based architectures — arise as restricted projections of this meta-
structure.
11


--- Page 12 ---
Typing of the structural components.
Each component of the meta-model θ = (r, H(θ), Π, L, E(θ), M(θ))
lives in a distinct mathematical space:
• r is a representation operator
r : Z →X,
mapping observations/interactions Z to an internal representational space X;
• H(θ) is a class of hypotheses (predictors, policies, or programs) defined over the representational
space induced by r;
• Π is a prior or inductive bias over H(θ) (e.g., a probability measure, description-length prior,
or structural regularizer);
• ℓis an evaluative functional
ℓ: H(θ) × E(θ) →R,
possibly multi-regime, encoding objectives and constraints;
• E(θ) is a class of admissible environments or task-generating processes (e.g., distributions,
MDPs, POMDPs);
• M(θ) is a structured memory space, supporting persistent internal state.
These components are structurally distinct and not interchangeable: they belong to different
mathematical categories (functions, sets, measures, functionals, process classes, state spaces).
Structural Semantics and Induced Dynamics.
In this article, θ is a purely structural meta-
model: it specifies what the state variables are, how they are typed, and which structural operators
are admissible, but it does not commit to a unique temporal evolution law.
Induced state space.
The typing of θ = (r, H(θ), Π, L, E(θ), M(θ)) determines canonical carrier
spaces for its components:
r ∈R(θ),
h ∈H(θ),
π ∈P(θ),
m ∈M(θ),
and therefore induces the structured state space
Sθ := R(θ) × H(θ) × P(θ) × M(θ).
A configuration at time t is a point
st = (rt, ht, πt, mt) ∈Sθ,
while E(θ) specifies the admissible class of environments/task-families in which trajectories are
generated.
12


--- Page 13 ---
Behavioral semantics as a set of compatible dynamics.
Let ∆(S) denote the set of probability
measures over S (the deterministic case corresponding to Dirac kernels). We define the space of
admissible stochastic learning dynamics as
D :=
[
S
n
T : S × Z →∆(S)
o
.
Because a structural θ may admit multiple compatible implementations, its behavioral semantics is
set-valued:
Sem : Θ ⇒D,
Sem(θ) ⊆
n
T : Sθ × Z →∆(Sθ)
o
.
An induced (chosen) dynamics compatible with θ is any selection
Tθ ∈Sem(θ).
Scope.
We deliberately keep the present paper focused on the structural axis; richer multi-
timescale or multi-operator semantics can be layered on top without altering the definitions below
(see Discussion).
SMGI lives on realizations.
Because Sem(θ) is set-valued, all verifiable claims are made at the
level of a realization (θ, T) with T ∈Sem(θ); we occasionally call θ SMGI-admissible when there
exists such a T satisfying the obligations defined later (Sec. 4).
Internal Stratification of Memory and Evaluation (Foundational Scope).
The present
meta-model treats M(θ) (memory) and evaluator family L (evaluative structure) as structured
components of θ, without fully stratifying their internal organization.
In companion work, memory is decomposed into interacting subsystems (working, episodic,
semantic, procedural, world-model, social/theory-of-mind, normative, and meta-memory layers),
each supporting distinct temporal and functional roles. Similarly, the evaluative structure may itself
admit multi-regime and hierarchical organization.
A full formal treatment of cross-memory interaction, multi-timescale dynamics, and regime
coordination constitutes an orthogonal axis of analysis. Such an axis would refine the behavioral
semantics Sem(θ) by introducing families of interacting transition operators indexed by memory
strata or temporal scales.
The present work isolates the minimal structural conditions under which a unified learning
system remains closed, stable, statistically controlled, and evaluatively coherent under admissible
task transformations. The stratified refinement of memory and evaluation extends — rather than
alters — these structural foundations.
Normative/value memory provides operational persistence of evaluative commitments defined at
the level of ℓ; it does not replace the formal evaluative structure but supports its temporal stability.
On the distinct roles of representation r and environment class E(θ).
A frequent ambiguity
in unified learning formalisms is whether the environment/task component can be absorbed into data,
or whether representation can be absorbed into the hypothesis class. In the present meta-model, r
and E(θ) are structurally distinct because they play different logical roles and live in different spaces.
Environment class. E(θ) denotes a class of admissible environments/tasks (e.g., distributions
over Z in supervised learning, or a class of MDP/POMDPs in interactive settings). Its purpose is to
define the domain of generality and the class T of admissible task transformations τ : E(θ) →E(θ)′.
Without an explicit E(θ), the notion of “closure under task transformation” is not well-typed: there
13


--- Page 14 ---
is no object on which τ can act, and no principled way to distinguish in-distribution learning from
genuine cross-task transformation.
Representation operators. r denotes representation operators mapping raw observations/interactions
to an internal space on which hypotheses and policies operate. It is not reducible to H(θ): the
hypothesis class is defined relative to the representational interface induced by r. Representation is
therefore a first-class structural component: it determines which invariants can be expressed and
controlled under transformations.
Structural coupling.
Task transformations τ ∈T act on E(θ) but induce a change in the
observation/interaction process, hence in the distribution or dynamics of r(Z) (or of latent transitions
in the represented space). Structural generalization precisely concerns conditions under which there
exists a nontrivial admissible set S∗such that the induced learning dynamics remains well-formed
and stable when E(θ) is transformed, while r preserves an invariant core sufficient to transfer across
task families.
Lemma 3.1 (Non-reducibility of r and E(θ) (structural roles)). In the meta-structure θ =
(r, H(θ), Π, L, E(θ), M(θ)), the components r and E(θ) are not interchangeable: (i) removing E(θ)
eliminates a well-typed notion of admissible task transformations τ : E(θ) →E(θ)′ and therefore
of closure; (ii) removing r precludes a typed statement of representational invariants and forces
invariance claims to be expressed only via direct hypotheses over raw Z or via implicit assumptions,
preventing a structural statement of invariance under transformations beyond trivial reparameteriza-
tions.
Structural Model of General Intelligence (SMGI).
We call the resulting formalization a
Structural Model of General Intelligence (SMGI). Formally, SMGI is a property of a compatible
pair (θ, Tθ), where θ ∈Θ is a purely structural meta-model and Tθ ∈Sem(θ) ⊆D is an induced
dynamics operator. We write (θ, Tθ) ∈SMGI iff the induced learning dynamics satisfies the four
structural requirements (i)–(iv) above.
Intuitively, these requirements ensure that:
• the system remains well-formed under task change (closure),
• its parameters and memory do not drift uncontrollably (stability),
• its hypothesis components remain statistically controllable (capacity),
• and its evaluation preserves a coherent invariant core (normative invariance).
We later prove that these four conditions are structurally minimal: removing any one of them
destroys at least one of the guarantees required for persistent cross-domain intelligence.
4
Formal Meta-Structure: Structural Generalization of Learning
Systems
4.1
From Classical Learning to Structural Learning
Classical statistical learning as a fixed-interface problem.
Canonical statistical learning
theory (SLT) formalizes learning under a fixed interface via a triple
(Z, H, ℓ),
14


--- Page 15 ---
where Z is the example space (e.g., Z = X × Y in supervised learning), H is a hypothesis class, and
ℓ: H × Z →R+ is a loss. Given a (typically stationary) data-generating distribution D over Z, the
goal is to control and minimize the expected risk
R(h) = Ez∼D
ℓ(h, z)
.
Structural Risk Minimization (SRM) refines this core by organizing hypotheses into nested classes
{Hk} with increasing capacity and selecting a suitable level via capacity control Vapnik [1998a].
PAC-Bayes and MDL further sharpen such control by making the inductive bias explicit through a
prior or description-length principle, yielding bounds of the schematic form
R(Q) ≲
bR(Q) +
s
KL(Q∥P) + ln(1/δ)
n
,
for a posterior Q relative to a prior P and n samples (details depend on the specific bound).
What classical extensions still keep fixed.
Modern learning theory includes powerful sequential
and non-stationary extensions—online learning, adaptive/dynamic regret, and learning under
distribution shift—that relax i.i.d. assumptions while keeping the learning interface conceptually
fixed (a specified hypothesis/update mechanism and a fixed evaluation semantics). For instance, non-
stationary online learning formalizes changing environments via dynamic/adaptive regret measures
[Zhao et al., 2024]. Similarly, PAC-Bayes has been extended to online/sequential settings, including
dependent data streams [Haddouche and Guedj, 2022], and recent work resolves long-standing
difficulties in sequential prior updates without information loss [Wu et al., 2024]. These advances
are essential but they typically treat the learning interface—representation, hypothesis carriers,
evaluator semantics, and memory operators—as exogenously fixed objects whose parameters evolve.
Structural learning: generalization over admissible interface change.
The motivating
gap addressed here is different: in agentic and tool-augmented systems, the learning problem is
often to update h while remaining well-formed and controllable under admissible changes of the
interface itself: task/environment families, tool interfaces, evaluators/norms, and memory/update
operators. We therefore lift the object of analysis from hypotheses to a typed meta-structure and
treat general intelligence as admissibility of the coupled dynamics under typed transformations.
4.2
Meta-Structure of a Learning System
The structural meta-model.
We model a persistent learning system by a structural tuple
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M),
where:
• r is a representation/interface operator (or family of operators);
• H denotes the hypothesis/program architecture admitted under r;
• Π is a structural prior / inductive bias / capacity regulator (SRM/MDL/PAC-Bayes style);
• L = {ℓk}K
k=1 is a family of evaluative regimes (multi-regime evaluation), with the active
evaluator ℓt possibly selected/weighted over L;
15


--- Page 16 ---
• E is the admissible environment/task family (data- or process-generating class);
• M is a structured memory space together with explicit write/consolidate/forget operators.
This elevates representation, evaluation, environment families, and memory to first-class typed com-
ponents. Compatibility with the induced state space Sθ and set-valued realization semantics Sem(θ)
is given in Sec. 3. The present section focuses on the structural axis: admissible transformations
and the obligations they must preserve.
Admissible (typed) transformations.
The distinctive ingredient of structural learning is an
explicit class T of admissible transformations (task/interface transformations). At minimum, T acts
on the environment/task family; more generally, it may also act on r, M, and the regime structure
of evaluation, provided typing is preserved.
Definition 4.1 (Topologically admissible task transformations). Equip E with a probability metric
DE (e.g., Wasserstein distance on task-generating distributions). A class of task transformations
T = {τ : E →E′} is topologically admissible for θ if there exists ϵmax < ∞such that
sup
e∈E
DE
␀e, τ(e)
 ≤ϵmax,
and the representation operator r admits a restricted (local) Lipschitz control on the admissible
region relevant to T : there exists Lr < ∞such that for all admissible e and all τ ∈T ,
dR
␀r(e), r(τ(e))
 ≤Lr DE
␀e, τ(e)
,
where dR is a metric on the representation space. (Importantly, this is a local/restricted requirement,
not a global Lipschitz constant over all inputs.) This restricts T to bounded structural perturbations,
ruling out discontinuous regime jumps that make learning/control ill-typed or non-auditable.
SMGI as admissibility of a coupled realization.
SMGI is not a property of θ alone but of a
compatible realization (θ, Tθ) (Sec. 3). We now state the SMGI admissibility obligations at the level
needed for subsequent guarantees.
Definition 4.2 (Structural Model of General Intelligence (SMGI)). Let θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) ∈Θ
be a structural meta-model and let Tθ be an induced (possibly stochastic) dynamics operator
compatible with θ (i.e., Tθ ∈Sem(θ) over the induced state space Sθ as in Sec. 3). We say that
(θ, Tθ) is an SMGI instance, written (θ, Tθ) ∈SMGI, if there exist:
• a nonempty admissible invariant set S∗
θ ⊆Sθ,
• a class T of admissible task/environment transformations τ : E →E′,
• and for each τ ∈T a compatible induced operator Tθ,τ (a semantics of execution/learning
under τ),
such that the following four requirements hold:
(i) Structural closure. For all τ ∈T ,
Tθ,τ(S∗
θ) ⊆S∗
θ,
i.e., admissible evolution remains well-formed under the task/interface changes claimed by the
system.
16


--- Page 17 ---
(ii) Dynamical stability. There exists a nonnegative Lyapunov-like witness V : Sθ →R+ and
constants B < ∞and λ ∈(0, 1] such that for every τ ∈T and every trajectory (st)t≥0
generated by Tθ,τ from any s0 ∈S∗
θ, a uniform non-explosion bound holds, e.g.
sup
t≥0
E[V (st)] ≤B V (s0) + B.
Equivalent certified stability notions may be used (contraction, input-to-state stability, or
martingale/supermartingale criteria). In control/RL settings, generalized Lyapunov certificates
provide a concrete route to such witnesses [Long et al., 2025].
(iii) Bounded statistical capacity along admissible evolution. There exists a complexity
functional Comp (VC/Rademacher, PAC-Bayes, MDL/Kolmogorov, etc.) such that capacity
remains uniformly controlled over admissible configurations, e.g.
sup
s∈S∗
θ
Comp(H(s)) < ∞,
where H(s) denotes the effective hypothesis/update component active at state s (possibly
state-dependent via r and Π). This is the structural analogue of uniform generalization control,
and it admits sequential/online variants via online PAC-Bayes and recursive prior updates
[Haddouche and Guedj, 2022, Wu et al., 2024].
(iv) Evaluative invariance across regime transformations. There exists a nontrivial invariant
evaluative constraint family (a protected “normative core”) C ⊆L such that along any
admissible trajectory under any τ ∈T , the induced evaluative state remains coherent on C,
for instance via invariance under a projection/restriction operator:
ΠC(ℓt+1) = ΠC(ℓt),
st ∈S∗
θ.
Minimality (structural indispensability).
The four requirements in Definition 4.2 are intended
as a minimal structural characterization: each requirement eliminates a distinct, unavoidable failure
mode of persistent cross-regime intelligence.
Theorem 4.3 (Minimality of the SMGI conditions). Let θ ∈Θ be a structural meta-model and let
Tθ be a compatible induced dynamics operator. Consider the four SMGI requirements (i)–(iv) of
Definition 4.2. Then each requirement is structurally indispensable in the following sense: for every
j ∈{(i), (ii), (iii), (iv)} there exist a transformation family T and a compatible realization (θ, Tθ)
that satisfies the other three requirements but violates j, and the corresponding defining guarantee
necessarily fails: without (i) the evolution is not well-formed under admissible task change; without
(ii) persistent drift/divergence occurs; without (iii) statistical control becomes vacuous via capacity
blow-up; without (iv) evaluative coherence/identity collapses under regime change.
Proof. We exhibit, for each requirement j ∈{(i), (ii), (iii), (iv)}, a concrete well-typed realization
(θ, Tθ) and a transformation family T that satisfies the other three requirements but violates j.
Each construction uses a simple state space and can be instantiated within the general meta-model
by freezing irrelevant components.
Failure of (i) (closure).
Let Sθ = {0, 1} and let Tθ,τ be the deterministic kernel Tθ,τ(s, z) = 1
for all inputs. Take S∗
θ = {0} and take any nontrivial transformation family T for which the induced
operator exists (typing is trivial here). Then Tθ,τ(S∗
θ) = {1} ⊈{0}, so (i) fails. Requirements
(ii)–(iv) can be made true vacuously by choosing a bounded Lyapunov witness V ≡0, a finite
hypothesis class (so capacity is bounded), and a fixed protected evaluative core (so evaluative
invariance holds).
17


--- Page 18 ---
Failure of (ii) (stability).
Let Sθ = N and define a deterministic dynamics Tθ,τ(s, z) = s + 1
(independent of τ), with S∗
θ = Sθ. Closure (i) holds for any T because S∗
θ is invariant. Choose a
finite hypothesis/update interface (so (iii) holds) and keep the evaluative core fixed (so (iv) holds).
However, for the canonical witness V (s) = s, we have V (st) = s0 + t and supt≥0 E[V (st)] = ∞, so
no Lyapunov-style non-explosion bound of Definition 4.2 can hold; hence (ii) fails.
Failure of (iii) (capacity).
Let Sθ be any bounded invariant set (e.g., Sθ = {0}) so that closure
(i) and stability (ii) hold trivially and let evaluators be fixed so that (iv) holds. Define the effective
hypothesis component at state s to be H(s) = Hn where n is an internal counter encoded in θ
and allowed to increase along admissible evolution (for example by letting θ include a component
nt →nt+1 = nt + 1 while keeping Sθ itself fixed). Choose Hn with strictly increasing complexity
(e.g., VC dimension VC(Hn) = n). Then sups∈S∗
θ Comp(H(s)) = ∞, so (iii) fails while (i),(ii),(iv)
hold.
Failure of (iv) (evaluative invariance).
Let Sθ be bounded and invariant and let the dynamics
be stable (e.g., Tθ,τ the identity kernel) so that (i) and (ii) hold. Let the hypothesis class be fixed and
finite so that (iii) holds. Define two regimes with evaluators ℓ1, ℓ2 and let T include a regime-switch
transformation that replaces the active evaluator by ℓ2 and violates a protected constraint Φ that
was satisfied under ℓ1. Concretely, take two hypotheses ha, hb and define ℓ1 and ℓ2 so that the
protected ordering on the core flips: Rℓ1(ha) < Rℓ1(hb) but Rℓ2(ha) > Rℓ2(hb). Then no nontrivial
protected core can remain invariant under this transformation, so (iv) fails while (i)–(iii) hold.
These four constructions show each requirement rules out a distinct failure mode and is therefore
structurally indispensable.
A nontrivial consequence: structural generalization under transformations.
We now
state a representative guarantee illustrating why the obligations are not merely definitional. It
exhibits the signature coupling of (i)–(iv): capacity control (PAC-Bayes/MDL) plus certified drift
control (Lyapunov) under admissible transformations.
Theorem 4.4 (Structural generalization bound for SMGI). Let θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) be an SMGI
system satisfying Definition 4.2. Fix a transformation τ ∈T and consider the induced dynamics
Tθ,τ restricted to S∗
θ. Assume:
1. (Bounded loss on S∗
θ) 0 ≤ℓτ(s, z) ≤1 for s ∈S∗
θ;
2. (Lyapunov drift control) there exists V and B < ∞such that supt≥0 E[V (st)] ≤BV (s0)+B
along admissible trajectories;
3. (PAC-Bayes complexity) the induced hypothesis/update mechanism admits a prior Pτ and
posterior Qτ with finite KL(Qτ∥Pτ), in a setting where sequential/online PAC-Bayes control
applies (e.g., via online PAC-Bayes or recursive prior updates [Haddouche and Guedj, 2022,
Wu et al., 2024]).
4. (Loss sensitivity to state) there exists L < ∞such that for all s, s′ ∈S∗
θ and all z,
|ℓτ(s, z) −ℓτ(s′, z)| ≤L |V (s) −V (s′)|.
18


--- Page 19 ---
Then for any δ ∈(0, 1), with probability at least 1 −δ over n observations (under the sampling
assumptions required by the chosen PAC-Bayes variant),
Rτ(Qτ) ≤ˆRτ(Qτ) +
v
u
u
tKL(Qτ∥Pτ) + ln 2√n
δ
2(n −1)
+ 2L
n
n−1
X
t=0
E[V (st)] .
In particular, using the Lyapunov bound,
Rτ(Qτ) ≤ˆRτ(Qτ) +
v
u
u
tKL(Qτ∥Pτ) + ln 2√n
δ
2(n −1)
+ 2L
␀BV (s0) + B
.
Proof. A complete proof is given in Appendix A, including an explicit martingale decomposition
and the separation of statistical and drift terms. Here we note the dependency structure. Closure (i)
restricts the analysis to the admissible invariant set S∗
θ where all quantities are well-defined under τ.
Capacity control (iii) provides the sequential PAC-Bayes term controlling the statistical component
of the generalization gap. Stability (ii) supplies a Lyapunov drift bound controlling the contribution
of stateful evolution to the generalization gap. Finally, evaluative invariance (iv) ensures that the
loss semantics used to define Rτ does not change adversarially along admissible evolution, so the
risk being bounded remains a fixed object across regime transformations.
4.3
Unifying Structural Guarantees Under θ
Objective.
The previous subsection isolated three complementary ingredients for non-vacuous
guarantees under structural evolution: (i) a typed admissible transformation class T (closure and well-
formedness under task/interface change), (ii) sequential capacity control (PAC-Bayes/MDL/SRM in
a form compatible with filtrations and dependence), and (iii) certified drift control of the induced
dynamics (Lyapunov-like witnesses on the coupled state space). This subsection packages these
ingredients into a single admissibility bundle, clarifying (a) which assumptions are structural
(statements about θ and typing), which are semantic/dynamical (statements about Tθ), and (b)
where evaluative invariance is logically necessary rather than merely interpretive.
Admissibility bundle and scope.
Fix a structural meta-model θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) and a
compatible induced dynamics Tθ ∈Sem(θ) acting on Sθ (Sec. 3). Let τ ∈T denote an admissible
task/interface transformation (typed in the sense of the paper), and let Tθ,τ denote a compatible
induced operator realizing the system under τ. We work on an admissible invariant set S∗
θ ⊆Sθ
(nonempty), and with the natural filtration Ft = σ(s0, z1, . . . , zt) along trajectories under Tθ,τ.
Definition 4.5 (Unified admissibility bundle). We say that (θ, Tθ) admits a unified structural
guarantee bundle (under T ) if there exist constants ϵmax, Lℓ, cV ≥0 and a witness V : Sθ →R+
such that for every τ ∈T :
(U1) Typed closure (well-formedness).
There exists a compatible realization Tθ,τ and a
nonempty invariant set S∗
θ such that Tθ,τ(S∗
θ) ⊆S∗
θ and all involved objects remain well-typed
under τ (in particular, r, H(θ), M(θ), and the evaluator interface remain defined).
(U2) Bounded transformation magnitude. The transformation is bounded in the environ-
ment/interface metric:
sup
e∈E(θ)
DE
␀e, τ(e)
 ≤ϵmax.
19


--- Page 20 ---
(U3) Lipschitz evaluative shift under τ. We allow state-conditioned evaluation and write
ℓτ(h; s, z) for the loss incurred at state s ∈Sθ on interaction token/transition z under τ. For
admissible (s, z) and all effective hypotheses h,
ℓτ(h; s, z) −ℓ(h; s, z)
 ≤Lℓ· DE
␀e, τ(e)
.
(Here ℓτ denotes the evaluator semantics in the transformed regime; if ℓis multi-regime, ℓτ is
the regime-indexed instance determined by τ.)
(U4) Certified stability (Lyapunov drift). Along trajectories (st) generated by Tθ,τ from
s0 ∈S∗
θ,
E[V (st+1) | Ft] ≤(1 −α) V (st) + β
for some α ∈(0, 1], β ≥0.
(U5) Sequential capacity control (PAC-Bayes/online admissibility).
There exists a
prior/posterior mechanism (Pt, Qt) adapted to (Ft) such that a sequential/online PAC-Bayes
generalization bound applies to the empirical trajectory risk under Tθ,τ (e.g., [Haddouche and
Guedj, 2022, Wu et al., 2024]), yielding a complexity term Genn(Qn, Pn, δ) that is finite for
the admissible trajectories considered.
Unified theorem: structural guarantees as a single implication.
The next statement is the
“one-line” unification: under the admissibility bundle, the core guarantees (closure, stability, capacity
control) compose into a single bound that remains meaningful under admissible transformations.
Evaluative invariance enters as a semantic well-posedness condition ensuring that the risk being
bounded is not a moving target.
Theorem 4.6 (Unified structural guarantee under admissible transformations). Assume (θ, Tθ)
admits the unified admissibility bundle of Definition 4.5 on a nonempty S∗
θ. Fix any τ ∈T and let
ˆRτ,n(Qn) denote the empirical trajectory risk under Tθ,τ over n steps, with corresponding expected
risk Rτ(Qn). Then for any δ ∈(0, 1), with probability at least 1 −δ,
Rτ(Qn) ≤ˆRτ,n(Qn) + Genn(Qn, Pn, δ) + Lℓϵmax + cV
n
n−1
X
t=0
E[V (st)].
(3)
Moreover, the Lyapunov drift condition implies a uniform stability bound supt≥0 E[V (st)] ≤V (s0) +
β/α, hence the stability term in (3) is uniformly bounded and does not explode with horizon.
Proof. Fix τ ∈T and work on the invariant set S∗
θ from (U1), so all objects are well-typed and
trajectories remain in S∗
θ. By (U5), a sequential/online PAC-Bayes inequality applies to the trajectory
risk in the τ-transformed regime, yielding with probability at least 1 −δ:
Rτ(Qn) ≤ˆRτ,n(Qn) + Genn(Qn, Pn, δ) + Driftn,
where Driftn denotes the additional term induced by stateful dependence.
To control the transformation-induced shift, use (U2)–(U3): for any admissible environment
instance e ∈E(θ), the evaluator shift satisfies |ℓτ −ℓ| ≤LℓDE(e, τ(e)) ≤Lℓϵmax. This contributes
the additive term Lℓϵmax.
To control Driftn, apply (U4): taking expectations and summing the drift inequality yields
supt≥0 E[V (st)] ≤V (s0) + β/α and therefore cV
n
Pn−1
t=0 E[V (st)] ≤cV (V (s0) + β/α). Substituting
these bounds into the PAC-Bayes inequality yields (3) and the stated uniform boundedness of the
stability term.
20


--- Page 21 ---
Where evaluative invariance is essential.
Theorem 4.6 bounds Rτ under a fixed evaluator
semantics in the transformed regime. In SMGI, evaluative invariance is the condition ensuring
that the meaning of the bounded risk is stable across regimes: if the evaluator is allowed to drift
arbitrarily under τ, then neither Rτ nor the protected constraint semantics are well-defined objects
of control, and the guarantee becomes vacuous (one can reduce risk by changing what “risk” means).
Consequently, evaluator updates must be either (i) disallowed under T on a protected core, or (ii)
permitted only through certificate-gated meta-transformations that preserve a declared invariant
evaluative core (as formalized elsewhere in the paper). This is the precise logical role of evaluative
invariance: it prevents the guarantee from collapsing into an artefact of metric drift.
Interpretation and compositionality.
Definition 4.5 is intentionally modular: closure ensures
that the semantics remains typed under T ; capacity control bounds statistical complexity in a
dependence-compatible manner; stability bounds the endogenous drift induced by memory and
sequential adaptation; and Lipschitz transformation control measures exogenous regime shift
magnitude. Together they yield (3), which can be specialized to classical regimes by freezing
components of θ or restricting T to identity transformations. This provides the formal bridge from
fixed-interface SLT to structural learning under admissible interface evolution.
Relation to non-stationary online learning.
Non-stationary online learning studies perfor-
mance under time-varying losses/environments, often via dynamic regret and interval-adaptive
regret [Zhao et al., 2024]. Our setting is complementary: the focus is not only on competing with
changing comparators, but on maintaining typed well-formedness, certificate stability, and evaluative
invariance under admissible task/interface transformations. In particular, the admissibility bundle
in Definition 4.5 makes explicit which parts of the interface are allowed to vary (via T ) and which
must remain certified, whereas most non-stationary formulations keep the learning interface fixed
and measure variability only at the level of losses or comparators.
4.4
Representation as Structural Condition
Structural role of representation in SMGI.
In SMGI, representation is not a cosmetic encoding
choice: it is a typed structural interface that determines (i) which regularities are expressible by
the hypothesis space H(θ), (ii) which invariances can be stated under admissible transformations
τ ∈T , and (iii) which guarantees remain meaningful when the learning interface evolves. Formally,
changes in representation induce changes in the effective hypothesis interface and thus affect closure
(i) and capacity (iii) as obligations on the coupled realization (θ, Tθ).
Typed representation map (notation coherence).
Throughout the paper, Z denotes the
carrier of observations/interactions (see Sec. 3 and the formal foundations), while X denotes the
internal representational space induced by the interface. Accordingly, representation is modeled as
the typed operator
r : Z −→X,
(4)
mapping raw interaction tokens/observations z ∈Z to internal structured representations x =
r(z) ∈X. This aligns the present subsection with the typing discipline used in the definition of Sθ
and Sem(θ).
Transformation-aligned representation as a closure witness.
A core SMGI requirement is
that representation remain comparable under admissible task/interface transformations. Let T be
21


--- Page 22 ---
the declared admissible transformation class acting on the task/environment side (and therefore
on the induced observation process). We say that r is T -aligned if there exists an induced action
Γ : T →End(X) such that for all admissible τ ∈T and admissible observations z ∈Z,
r(τ · z) = Γ(τ)
␀r(z)
.
(5)
When Γ(τ) = Id this is invariance; when Γ(τ) is an automorphism it is equivariance. Condition
(5) is the minimal typed statement that makes closure under T operational at the representational
level.
Representation selection as a meta-level structural objective.
Unlike classical feature
engineering, r is not assumed fixed. Its selection is part of the structural degrees of freedom in θ. We
model this via a task/transformation-weighted objective: let PT be a distribution over admissible
transformations (or, equivalently, over the induced task instances they generate), and let G(τ, r)
denote a typed structural objective measuring, for example, predictive risk under τ, invariance
violation, and/or a structural cost of the interface. We write the meta-selection problem as
r∗= arg min
r∈R Eτ∼PT
 G(τ, r)
,
(6)
where R is a typed admissible class of representation operators. Equation (6) does not commit to a
particular learning algorithm; it exposes that representation is a structural commitment that must
be evaluated jointly with H(θ), Π, and evaluator family L.
Structural interpretation (why “representation is a condition of intelligibility”).
In
SMGI terms, representation is a condition of intelligibility precisely because it fixes which invariants
can be expressed and preserved under admissible structural evolution: alignment (5) contributes
directly to closure (i), while restrictions on R and the induced hypothesis interface contribute to
capacity control (iii). This provides the formal content behind the philosophical motivation: changes
in r change the induced ontology and therefore what counts as stable generalization under T .
4.5
Generalized Structural Risk Minimization
From SRM to structural SRM.
Classical Structural Risk Minimization (SRM) controls
generalization by selecting a hypothesis from a nested hierarchy of classes while keeping the learning
interface fixed [Vapnik, 1998a]. In SMGI, the interface itself carries typed degrees of freedom
(representation, evaluator regimes, memory operators), so the analogue of SRM must control not
only hypothesis complexity but also structural degrees of freedom activated across regimes and
time. We therefore define a generalized objective in which (i) the evaluator is multi-regime, (ii)
regime switching is explicit and costly, and (iii) coherence constraints enforce admissibility of regime
configurations.
Typed regimes and task-conditioned risk.
Let R = {1, . . . , K} index evaluative regimes
and let L = {ℓk}K
k=1 denote the corresponding family of regime evaluators (as used elsewhere in
the paper). For each task/environment instance τ (or equivalently for each induced environment
distribution Dτ over z ∈Z), define the regime-specific risk
Rτ,k(h) := Ez∼Dτ
ℓk(h, z)
.
(7)
(When the system is stateful, the same definition applies to trajectory-dependent losses by condi-
tioning on the induced dynamics; Eq. (7) is the canonical static shorthand.)
22


--- Page 23 ---
Contextual regime selection as a typed operator.
Let ct denote a context descriptor (e.g.,
an element of a context space C) and let st ∈Sθ denote the structural state of the system at time t
(Sec. 3). We model regime selection by a typed stochastic switching operator
kt ∼σ(· | ct, st),
σ : C × Sθ →∆(R),
(8)
where ∆(R) denotes the probability simplex over regimes. This makes explicit that regime choice
is part of the induced semantics Tθ (it depends on the evolving state), and therefore is subject to
SMGI stability and invariance constraints.
Structural objective over a horizon.
Let (ht)T
t=1 denote the effective hypothesis/policy com-
ponent selected by the induced dynamics, and (zt)T
t=1 the interaction stream generated under the
task/environment process. We define a generalized structural SRM objective over horizon T:
min
θ
E
" T
X
t=1

ℓkt(ht, zt) + α CostSwitch(kt−1, kt) + β Incoh(kt; K)
#
,
(9)
where:
• CostSwitch : R × R →R+ penalizes excessive regime volatility (e.g., to prevent pathological
oscillations that would break stability (ii) or evaluative coherence (iv));
• Incoh(·; K) penalizes structurally forbidden regime configurations relative to a declared co-
herence constraint set K (e.g., logical/normative incompatibilities, forbidden combinations of
constraints, or protected-core violations).
The expectation in (9) is taken over the trajectory induced jointly by the environment process, the
switching operator (8), and the induced dynamics Tθ.
Interpretation and relation to SMGI obligations.
Equation (9) makes the key structural
commitments explicit: (i) closure requires that the regime-selection operator and the evaluator family
remain well-typed under admissible transformations; (ii) stability constrains switching and memory
interaction so that the induced dynamics does not drift uncontrollably; (iii) capacity constrains the
effective complexity of the joint interface (representation, hypothesis class, switching, memory); (iv)
evaluative invariance is enforced by restricting K to preserve a protected evaluative core, and by
making regime updates admissible only under certified constraints (as formalized elsewhere in the
paper).
Classical SRM as a special case.
Classical SRM is recovered as the special case K = 1 with
deterministic regime selection, σ(· | ct, st) ≡δ1, CostSwitch ≡0, and Incoh ≡0. In this sense,
generalized structural SRM extends SRM by lifting “capacity control” from hypothesis hierarchies
alone to the full structural interface controlled by θ.
4.6
PAC-Bayes Meta-Level Control
Role of PAC-Bayes in SMGI.
In classical PAC-Bayes, the prior regulates hypothesis complexity
under a fixed interface. In SMGI, the goal is stronger: capacity control must remain meaningful under
admissible interface evolution (representation changes, regime switching, and memory-mediated
dynamics). Accordingly, we treat the prior not merely as a regularizer over parameters, but as a
structural prior that constrains admissible configurations of the learning interface itself.
23


--- Page 24 ---
Typed objects and what is randomized.
Fix a task/environment instance τ (or an induced en-
vironment distribution Dτ over interactions z ∈Z). Let H(θ) denote the effective hypothesis/policy
class induced by the current structural interface, and let Qτ denote a posterior distribution over
hypotheses (or hypothesis parameters) within H(θ). We write the task risk in the canonical form
Rτ(h) = Ez∼Dτ
ℓ(h, z)
,
and the posterior (Gibbs) risk
Rτ(Qτ) := Eh∼Qτ
Rτ(h)
.
Classical PAC-Bayes bound (baseline form).
For a prior Π over hypotheses and a posterior
Qτ, a standard PAC-Bayes inequality yields, with probability at least 1 −δ,
Rτ(Qτ) ≤
bRτ(Qτ) +
s
KL(Qτ ∥Π) + log 1
δ
2n
,
(10)
where bRτ(Qτ) is the empirical risk computed from n samples (or n interaction steps under the
sampling assumptions of the chosen PAC-Bayes variant). Equation (10) is included here as the
baseline form; the paper’s structural contribution is to specify what Π controls and how the bound
is used under structural evolution.
Structural prior as meta-capacity control.
In SMGI, Π is not merely a prior over parameter
vectors: it is a structural prior that regulates the admissible interface degrees of freedom. Concretely,
Π may be understood as a prior over a structural hypothesis space
Θstruct ⊇(r, H, L),
so that complexity penalties constrain (i) representational interface families r, (ii) induced hypothesis
interfaces H, and (iii) multi-regime evaluator families L (or the evaluator parameters governing
regime weights/selection). This is the precise sense in which PAC-Bayes becomes a meta-capacity
control mechanism: it bounds effective complexity of the interface, not only of a fixed predictor
class.
Sequential/online PAC-Bayes (compatibility with filtrations).
When the induced dynamics
Tθ generates dependent trajectories (memory, tool-use, regime switching), i.i.d. sampling is not
appropriate. SMGI therefore relies on sequential/online PAC-Bayes formalisms that are adapted to
filtrations and streaming data, so that the complexity term remains meaningful under dependence
and continual updates. In particular, online PAC-Bayes bounds extend PAC-Bayes reasoning to
online learning settings with dependent data streams, and recent recursive PAC-Bayes constructions
enable sequential prior updates without information loss. We cite these as modern instances of
PAC-Bayes-compatible capacity control under sequential evolution [Haddouche and Guedj, 2022,
Wu et al., 2024].
Connection to the unified admissibility bundle.
Within the unified guarantee bundle
(Definition 4.5), the PAC-Bayes term KL(Q∥Π) and its sequential analogue provide the capacity
component (iii), while stability witnesses control drift and evaluator invariance prevents the objective
from becoming a moving target. Thus PAC-Bayes meta-level control is not a standalone guarantee:
it is the capacity-control module of a structurally admissible coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ).
24


--- Page 25 ---
4.7
Memory and Forgetting as Operational Compression
Memory as a typed state component.
Let M(θ) denote the typed memory state space induced
by the structural interface θ, and let mt ∈M(θ). In SMGI, memory is not a passive store: it is an
explicit component of the coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ) whose evolution must remain well-formed under
admissible transformations and certified stability constraints.
Memory update and functional forgetting as explicit operators.
Memory state evolves
via a typed update operator
mt+1 = U(mt, zt, kt),
where zt ∈Z is the interaction stream and kt ∈{1, . . . , K} indexes the active evaluative regime. We
view these as typed maps U : M(θ)×Z×{1, . . . , K} →M(θ) and F : M(θ)×{1, . . . , K}×C →M(θ)
for a context space C. We introduce functional forgetting as an explicit, context- and regime-
conditioned operator
˜mt+1 = F(mt+1; kt, ct),
where ct ∈C is a context descriptor (e.g., security regime, deployment mode, audit mode). This
makes forgetting a first-class structural map rather than an implementation side effect.
Verification-oriented perspectives on machine unlearning clarify how forgetting can be made
auditable via behavioral or parametric evidence [Xue et al., 2025, Blanco-Justicia et al., 2025].
Forgetting as constrained compression (MDL viewpoint).
We define forgetting as con-
strained compression by selecting F to trade off task performance (under the active evaluative
regime) against a complexity penalty:
F ∗= arg min
F

Losskt(at | ˜mt+1) + λ Comp( ˜mt+1)

.
This connects directly to MDL and Kolmogorov complexity: compression implements an operational
simplicity bias and controls the effective capacity of the memory-bearing system [Li and Vitányi,
2008].
SMGI alignment: capacity, stability, and evaluative invariance.
Within SMGI, forgetting
is admissible only if it simultaneously supports the three obligations that interact with memory:
(i) capacity control by reducing the effective complexity of memory-dependent hypotheses via
Comp( ˜mt+1); (ii) stability by preventing uncontrolled drift or interference in the coupled dynamics
(memory is a feedback state, so forgetting acts as a stabilizing operator); and (iii) evaluative
invariance by preserving any protected evaluative core under regime switching (forgetting must
not “solve” constraints by erasing the information required to enforce them). A sufficient stability-
oriented design condition is to require F to be non-expansive (or contractive) under a chosen stability
witness/metric on M(θ), so that memory updates cannot amplify drift across regimes. This is
precisely why forgetting is modeled as an explicit operator F(·; kt, ct) rather than implicit parameter
decay.
Why “forgetting” is a modern control problem (2024–2026).
Recent continual-learning
work treats selective forgetting and memory refresh as a controllable mechanism rather than merely
a failure mode, including settings where test-time data can refresh or reshape memory without labels
[Singh et al., 2024]. In parallel, the growing literature on digital forgetting and machine unlearning
in large models emphasizes that forgetting must often be auditable and verifiable, not only heuristic
25


--- Page 26 ---
[Blanco-Justicia et al., 2025, Xue et al., 2025]. Finally, in agentic deployments, persistent memory
and retrieval stores introduce a concrete security attack surface (memory/RAG poisoning), making
controlled forgetting part of robustness and controllability rather than optional optimization [Jing
et al., 2025].
Interpretation.
Forgetting is not erasure; it is selective operational reduction. In SMGI terms,
