F is an admissible structural operator whose role is to compress memory while preserving certified
invariants, thereby enabling long-horizon stability and capacity control under regime shifts.
4.8
Program Prior Option (Typed Description-Length Prior)
A typed description language for structural objects.
Fix a typed description language
(or grammar) GΘ whose well-formed strings encode admissible meta-models θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M)
together with declared typing information. Let code(θ) ∈{0, 1}∗be a chosen encoding and define
the (computable) description length |θ| := |code(θ)|.
Structural description-length prior.
We define the optional structural prior
Π(θ) ∝2−|θ|
equivalently
Π(θ) ∝exp(−λ|θ|),
(11)
for some λ > 0 (absorbing constant factors). This is an MDL-style proxy for algorithmic simplicity:
it does not assume access to Kolmogorov complexity, but it produces a falsifiable pressure toward
structurally parsimonious interfaces.
What this prior controls (and what it does not).
The prior (11) regularizes interface
complexity: it penalizes representational degrees of freedom (r), hypothesis/interface choices (H),
memory/operator interfaces (M), and regime structure (L) when these are treated as selectable
components. It does not impose a normative hierarchy between evaluators: protected evaluative
invariance is handled separately by obligation (iv) and its certificate-gated update rules.
PAC-Bayes linkage as an explicit inequality.
Let Q be any posterior over structural objects
θ supported on well-typed models. Then, up to an additive normalizing constant cΠ, the KL term
expands as:
KL(Q∥Π) = Eθ∼Q[ |θ| ] · ln 2 + cΠ −H(Q),
(12)
where H(Q) is the Shannon entropy of Q (in nats). Thus, PAC-Bayes complexity control directly
upper-bounds expected structural description length, making obligation (iii) (bounded capacity) a
checkable meta-level regularization statement.
Interpretation.
This option can be switched off by restricting Π to a fixed structural family;
when enabled, it provides a transparent and implementation-agnostic capacity module for structural
evolution.
4.9
Theorem: Structural Strict Inclusion
Equivalence notion (evaluation-relative).
To avoid vacuity, we fix an evaluation-relative
notion of equivalence: two learning systems are said to be core-equivalent if, for every admissible
task/regime instance (or every τ ∈T in scope), they induce the same risk ordering on the protected
evaluative core (cf. obligation (iv)). This prevents “reductions” that merely redefine what counts as
risk.
26


--- Page 27 ---
Classical learners as degenerate SMGI instances.
Let Aclassical denote learning systems
with a fixed interface (r, H, ℓ) and no explicit regime structure (i.e., K = 1), and let ASG denote
systems represented by the SMGI meta-structure θ with typed operators. Every classical learner
embeds into SMGI by the special-case choice K = 1, σ(· | ct, st) ≡δ1,F ≡Id, and by restricting T
to identity transformations (or to the same task family used classically).
Theorem 4.7 (Strict structural inclusion). Under the above embedding, Aclassical ⊆ASG. Moreover,
the inclusion is strict: there exist SMGI-admissible configurations with K > 1 and typed regime
switching that are not core-equivalent to any classical single-regime system.
Proof. Inclusion. Let S ∈Aclassical be any fixed-interface learner with tuple (r, H, ℓ) and no regime
structure. Embed it into SMGI by choosing K = 1 (so L = {ℓ}), taking the regime selector σ to be
the constant Dirac distribution on the unique regime, and taking the forgetting operator (if present)
to be the identity. Restrict the admissible transformation class T to identities (or to the same
classical task family). Under this embedding, the induced semantics coincides with the classical
learner, hence Aclassical ⊆ASG.
Strictness. Consider an SMGI configuration with K = 2 regimes and two hypotheses/policies
ha, hb such that R1(ha) < R1(hb) but R2(ha) > R2(hb), where Rk denotes risk under evaluator ℓk.
Assume, for contradiction, that there exists a single-regime classical system with evaluator ℓ⋆that
is core-equivalent to this K = 2 configuration in the sense of the paper (i.e., it induces the same
protected risk ordering on the core for all admissible regimes). Then ℓ⋆must rank ha better than
hb (to match regime 1) and simultaneously rank hb better than ha (to match regime 2), which is
impossible for a single fixed ordering induced by one evaluator. Hence no K = 1 classical system
can be core-equivalent to this K = 2 SMGI instance, proving strictness.
5
Formal Foundations
This section fixes the minimal mathematical substrate used by the main results. It is intentionally
compact: the goal is not to re-teach learning theory, but to make every later claim well-typed,
checkable, and non-programmatic.
5.1
Measurable spaces, stochastic kernels, and trajectories
Let (Z, A) be the measurable interaction space (tokens, observations, transitions). Let (Sθ, S) be
the measurable state space induced by θ (Sec. 3).
An induced (possibly stochastic) learning-and-interaction dynamics compatible with θ is a
Markov kernel
Tθ,τ : (Sθ × Z, S ⊗A) →(Sθ, S),
where τ ∈T denotes an admissible task/interface transformation. Trajectories (st)t≥0 are generated
by iterating Tθ,τ on (st, zt+1).
We write Ft = σ(s0, z1, . . . , zt) for the natural filtration.
5.2
Metrics, non-expansiveness, and contraction
Assume (Sθ, dS) is a complete metric space and (M(θ), dM) is a complete metric subspace. A map
G : (X, d) →(X, d) is: (i) non-expansive if d(Gx, Gy) ≤d(x, y); (ii) a contraction if d(Gx, Gy) ≤
γd(x, y) for some γ ∈(0, 1).
These notions are used as checkable sufficient conditions for invariance (closure) and stability.
27


--- Page 28 ---
5.3
Lyapunov drift conditions for stateful learning
A (Foster–Lyapunov) drift condition is specified by a measurable witness V : Sθ →R+ and constants
α ∈(0, 1], β ≥0 such that for trajectories under Tθ,τ:
E[V (st+1) | Ft] ≤(1 −α) V (st) + β.
This implies uniform moment bounds on V (st) and prevents uncontrolled drift under sequential
adaptation.
5.4
Sequential PAC-Bayes as a capacity module
Assumption 5.1 (Sequential PAC-Bayes module (filtration-adapted)). Fix an admissible transfor-
mation τ ∈T and the induced filtration Ft = σ(s0, z1, . . . , zt) along trajectories under Tθ,τ. Assume
the instantaneous loss used in the bound is measurable, Ft-adapted, and uniformly bounded (e.g.,
in [0, 1]). Assume there exist prior/posterior processes Pt, Qt over the effective hypothesis/update
component that are Ft-adapted and satisfy the technical conditions of a sequential/online PAC-Bayes
inequality for dependent data streams (e.g., the online PAC-Bayes setting of Haddouche and Guedj
[2022] and/or recursive prior-update constructions such as Wu et al. [2024]), yielding with probability
at least 1 −δ a bound of the form
Rτ(Qn) ≤
bRτ,n(Qn) + Genn(Qn, Pn, δ),
where Genn contains a KL-type complexity term (possibly cumulative/iterated in time) and is finite
on admissible trajectories. We use this assumption as a capacity module for SMGI-(iii).
6
Capacity Analysis: Structural Generalization and Meta-Complexity
This section isolates the meta-level capacity question induced by structural evolution. Classical
capacity control (VC/Rademacher/SRM, PAC-Bayes, MDL) bounds generalization for learning
dynamics that operate inside a fixed interface. In SMGI, admissible evolution may modify parts of
the interface (representation, regime structure, and memory operators), so capacity control must
apply to the structural configuration itself.
6.1
Structural capacity as interface complexity
Fix a realization (θ, Tθ) with θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) and an admissible invariant set S∗
θ. We define
a structural complexity functional that decomposes across the typed components that may vary
under admissible evolution:
C(θ) := Cr(r) + CH
␀H(θ)
 + CL(L) + CM
␀M(θ)
,
(13)
where each term is instantiated by the chosen capacity formalism (e.g., VC/Rademacher for H(θ), a
switching/description cost for L, and an MDL-style or algorithmic-complexity proxy for memory
operators and state).
A minimal admissibility requirement for the capacity obligation (SMGI-(iii)) is that C(θ) remain
uniformly bounded on admissible configurations:
sup
s∈S∗
θ
C
␀θ(s)
 < ∞,
(14)
28


--- Page 29 ---
where θ(s) denotes the effective structural configuration active at state s (e.g., via state-conditioned
regime selection and memory policies).
Computability note. When CM (or the MDL/program-length proxy underlying it) is interpreted in an
algorithmic-information sense, the exact Kolmogorov description length is in general non-computable;
any future implementation must therefore rely on computable upper bounds or practical MDL-style
surrogates as strictly necessary approximations.
6.2
Description-length option (MDL / program-prior view)
To make (13) checkable without committing to a single parametric notion, one may adopt a typed
description language for structural objects and use a computable description-length proxy |θ| (e.g., an
MDL codelength, a prefix-free code length, or any certified upper bound on a description complexity).
This yields the optional structural simplicity principle (cf. Sec. 4.8):
θ∗= arg min
θ
h
bR(θ) + λ |θ|
i
,
(15)
where |θ| is a computable surrogate (not the exact Kolmogorov complexity, which is non-computable
in general). This is compatible with PAC-Bayes via the standard KL–code-length linkage (e.g.,
Eq. (12)). In this reading, regime plurality and memory operators increase expressivity, but only
those expansions that remain complexity-controlled are admissible under SMGI-(iii).
6.3
Link to Structural Risk and PAC-Bayes Guarantees
The capacity analysis developed in this section relies on the structural risk control and PAC-Bayesian
meta-level guarantees established earlier in Section 4.3 and the subsequent subsections.
We therefore do not re-derive these results here. Instead, we analyze their implications in terms
of meta-complexity: namely, how maintaining bounded structural risk and valid PAC-Bayes control
constrains the growth of representational, policy, and memory capacity under structural expansion
of environment families.
7
Positioning within Existing Theoretical Frameworks
The present structural meta-model is not proposed in isolation. It must be situated relative to
existing formal, architectural, epistemic, and large-scale empirical approaches to general intelligence.
Rather than comparing systems at the level of benchmark performance, we compare research
programs at the level of the structural meta-models they induce and the semantic constraints they
impose.
Programs as restrictions of the structural–dynamical universe.
Throughout this section, a
research program P is interpreted as inducing a restricted class of well-typed structural meta-models
Θ(P) ⊆Θ,
together with a restricted (set-valued) realization semantics
SemP : Θ(P) ⇒D,
SemP(θ) ⊆Sem(θ) ⊆D,
where D denotes the global class of admissible induced dynamics, and Sem is the general realization
semantics defined in Section 3. Equivalently, P induces the restricted semantic graph
Graph(SemP) := {(θ, T) ∈Θ(P) × D : T ∈SemP(θ)},
29


--- Page 30 ---
which satisfies
Graph(SemP) ⊆Graph(Sem).
Thus, program-level realizations are treated as restrictions of the general structural–dynamical
semantics.
Methodological consequence: a non-rhetorical comparison criterion.
We never claim that
a research program P “satisfies SMGI”. Instead, comparative analysis reduces to a single testable
criterion: whether P admits at least one realization satisfying the SMGI obligations, i.e.,
Graph(SemP) ∩SMGI ̸= ∅.
This separation prevents conflating capability, representation, and stability, and forces dynamical and
evaluative guarantees to be stated explicitly rather than inferred from scale or empirical performance.
The framework thus converts heterogeneous AGI paradigms into a unified admissibility problem
over the shared structural space Θ, replacing narrative comparison with a mathematically checkable
inclusion/intersection condition.
7.1
Why Positioning is Needed: Fragmented Axes and No Joint Object
The contemporary AGI landscape is characterized by a proliferation of highly successful but axis-
specific theories. Statistical learning theory formalizes generalization under sampling assumptions;
reinforcement learning formalizes sequential decision-making; algorithmic information theory formal-
izes universal prediction; meta-learning addresses cross-task adaptation; causal and epistemic logics
formalize structured reasoning; large-scale neural architectures unify heterogeneous modalities.
However, these frameworks do not specify a single mathematical object that jointly coordinates
representation, hypothesis evolution, update operators, memory stratification, and evaluation
constraints. Each axis is formalized in isolation. What remains under-specified is the level at which
these axes must be coordinated inside a single persistent system that remains well-formed under
admissible transformations.
This motivates an explicit positioning: not to rank programs by “AGI-ness,” but to articulate
the structural obligations required for their mechanisms to coexist within a unified meta-model.
7.2
From Fragmented Theories to a Meta-Model: The SMGI View
The SMGI framework provides a unified language to analyze the structural restrictions of current
paradigms. Positioning a research program P within this axis consists of identifying which compo-
nents of the structural interface are held fixed (degenerate cases, i.e., admissible transformations act
as the identity on those components) and which are allowed to evolve under certified updates.
Formally, P induces a restricted structural subclass Θ(P) ⊆Θ together with a restricted realiza-
tion semantics SemP, hence a restricted semantic graph Graph(SemP) ⊆Θ(P) × D. Comparisons
are then formulated parsimoniously by testing the non-emptiness of the intersection between program
realizations and the SMGI obligations:
Graph(SemP) ∩SMGI ̸= ∅.
(16)
As will be detailed in Section 7, classical ERM is recovered as a special case where the rep-
resentational interface and evaluator are fixed, while standard RL typically fixes the evaluative
structure to a single reward functional; SMGI highlights that stability and invariance properties
under task/interface transformations are not guaranteed by these formalisms unless made explicit
as obligations on the coupled dynamics.
30


--- Page 31 ---
7.3
Comparative Structural Overview
To clarify the structural contribution of the present work, we summarize how major theoretical
frameworks relate to key properties of a unified AGI meta-model in Table 1. We deliberately compare
heterogeneous paradigms (theories, architectures, and cognitive hypotheses) only along a single
axis: whether the paradigm makes the SMGI-relevant structural objects and obligations explicit
(as first-class components and constraints), rather than treating them as implicit assumptions or
external modules.
Reading guide.
Table 1 compares formalisms by whether they make SMGI-relevant objects
explicit; Table 3 then summarizes which SMGI obligations are structurally explicit in major AGI
program classes. In both cases, the goal is diagnostic (explicitness), not a claim of satisfaction.
7.4
Methodological Lineage of Meta-Models
A methodological precedent for meta-structural formalization comes from the formal sciences of
software and systems design. In model-driven engineering, a meta-model specifies (i) the class of
well-formed models (via a conformance relation), (ii) the admissible transformations between them,
and (iii) the invariants and constraints that must be preserved under such transformations [Object
Management Group, 2019, 2017]. This is not an analogy for style: it is a mature methodology for
making admissibility, closure under transformation, and invariant constraints explicit at the level of
system definition, rather than leaving them implicit in implementations.
Concretely, MOF makes explicit the meta-level at which a modeling language is defined, and
UML specifies a structured language of objects, relations, inheritance, and behavioral interfaces; in
both cases, correctness is ensured by constraints that apply to families of models rather than only to
individual instances [Object Management Group, 2019, 2017]. Object-oriented foundations similarly
show that inheritance, refinement, and subtyping are formal constraints governing admissible
transformations while preserving type-level invariants [Meyer, 1988, Booch, 1994].
In the present work, SMGI adopts the same methodological stance in a strictly formal sense.
The claim is methodological: we import the discipline of explicit well-formedness, admissible
transformations, and invariant constraints, without assuming that learning systems inherit the
determinism or completeness typical of engineered meta-models. A structural meta-model θ ∈Θ
defines an admissible class of learning-system descriptions, while its semantics Sem(θ) fixes which
induced learning dynamics and task/regime transformations are meaningful. In this perspective,
“well-formedness” corresponds to semantic admissibility (i.e., membership in Graph(Sem)), and
“model transformation” corresponds to task/environment operators acting on the semantic side
while respecting the invariants required by SMGI.
SMGI therefore does not propose a single architecture. It makes explicit a meta-level admissibility
space together with invariants that must be preserved under task and regime transformations, so
that heterogeneous mechanisms can be composed inside a single persistent learning system.
Stratification Levels and Meta-Modeling Hierarchy.
Model-driven engineering further
distinguishes multiple levels of abstraction (M0–M3) in which instances, models, meta-models, and
meta-meta-models are formally separated [Object Management Group, 2019]. A similar stratification
clarifies the present framework.
Concrete learning trajectories and executions correspond to instance-level realizations (analogous
to M0). Specific implemented agent architectures correspond to model-level descriptions (M1). The
structural tuple θ ∈Θ plays the role of a meta-model (M2), defining the admissible structural
31


--- Page 32 ---
language of learning systems. Finally, the theory specifying Θ together with its realization semantics
Sem plays a meta-meta role (analogous to M3), defining the admissibility space in which structural
meta-models themselves are well-formed.
This stratification is not an analogy for exposition; it ensures that structural definitions,
admissibility constraints, and transformation laws are specified at the appropriate abstraction level
rather than conflated with implementation details.
7.5
Meta-Logical and Symbolic Foundations for Structural Admissibility
The methodological stance of SMGI (and its M0–M3 stratification) is not merely expository. It is
motivated by constraints that arise when a persistent learning system must (i) revise parts of its own
learning interface, while (ii) preserving certified invariants under task and regime transformations.
We summarize four complementary foundations that justify treating typed structure, admissible
transformations, and invariant-preserving updates as first-class objects.
(I) Meta-logical necessity: typed ascent for specification and verification.
A classical
limitation of self-referential systems is that semantic notions such as truth, consistency, or correctness
of an object-language cannot, in general, be fully expressed and validated within that same language
without collapsing levels [Tarski, 1936]. In learning systems, the risk is not a literal paradox but an
untyped collapse: attempting to revise the evaluator ℓor the admissible update rules using only
object-level operators can make it unclear which obligations are being preserved, relative to what
semantics, and under which admissibility constraints. SMGI addresses this by treating the structural
interface θ as a typed meta-description for the induced dynamics Tθ, so that interface revisions are
represented as elements of a typed transformation class and subjected to explicit certificate/monitor
conditions (cf. Sec. 2.2).
(II) Systemic necessity: hierarchical decomposability and evolvability.
Complex adap-
tive systems evolve efficiently when organized around stable intermediate subsystems and nearly-
decomposable interfaces [Simon, 1962]. In SMGI, the components (r, H, Π, L, E, M) play the role
of typed sub-assemblies: they specify what may vary, what must remain invariant, and what must
be checked during structural evolution. This targets the failure mode of non-stratified approaches
where behavioral adaptation is conflated with admissible change of the interface itself: SMGI makes
evolvability a property of certified transformations on (θ, Tθ) rather than an informal expectation
from scale.
(III) Structural subsumption: degeneracy as restricting typed degrees of freedom.
The positioning criterion of Sec. 7 can be sharpened by interpreting many classical paradigms as
restricted instances obtained by freezing (or restricting) specific structural degrees of freedom. A
compact formal vocabulary for such restrictions is provided by category-theoretic forgetful mappings
that preserve only part of the available structure [Mac Lane, 1971]. We emphasize that this is an
optional formal viewpoint: it is used to express, concisely, that a program class corresponds to a
restricted realization semantics that keeps selected components fixed. For example, canonical RL
can be viewed as restricting the evaluator to a scalar reward and treating representational plasticity
as exogenous:
U(θSMGI) = (h, Lfixed) ∈PRL,
Lfixed = {ℓfixed}.
(17)
In this sense, “degenerate” means “structurally constrained” rather than inferior: it expresses that
SMGI measures expressiveness at the level of admissible structural transformations, not only at the
32


--- Page 33 ---
level of parameterized function classes.
(IV) Symbolic and computational grounding: typed hypotheses, explicit memory, and
contracts.
The explicit typing of θ provides clean interfaces to symbolic and neuro-symbolic
instantiations, without committing SMGI to any one substrate. First, H(θ) can be instantiated as a
space of relational or programmatic hypotheses, connecting structural generalization to ILP-style
structural synthesis [Muggleton, 1991b, Cropper and Dumančić, 2022, Dumančić et al., 2024]. Second,
M(θ) admits realizations as queryable structured memory systems, including knowledge-graph or
description-logic stores [Baader et al., 2003, Pan et al., 2024], which makes write/consolidate/forget
operators explicit objects rather than implicit side effects. Third, the MDE/PLT stance treats θ as
a specification layer and Tθ as an implementation layer: admissible updates correspond to contract-
respecting transformations, supported by model conformance and type-safety principles [Object
Management Group, 2019, Meyer, 1988, Pierce, 2002]. This aligns with verification-guided agent
control and guardrail enforcement [Corsi and et al., 2024, Dong et al., 2024b], while internalizing
the relevant objects (evaluators, invariants, admissible transformations) directly into the typed
meta-model.
Synthesis: structural continuity under interface change.
Finally, certified evolution must
control the transition itself: interface updates θ →θ′ should preserve a minimal certified core during
the transition, to avoid intervals where monitors or invariants are undefined. This motivates modeling
interface evolution with explicit continuity constraints, in the spirit of correctness conditions studied
for dynamic software updating [Hicks et al., 2005]. In SMGI terms, such constraints are expressed
as admissibility conditions on typed meta-transformations, ensuring that certified invariants remain
meaningful along the evolution of the coupled dynamics (θ, Tθ) and enabling subsequent stability
analysis.
7.6
SMGI Instantiation Map
To relate SMGI to contemporary AGI programs without over-claim, we adopt a conservative
structural inclusion schema.
A research program P determines a class of admissible implementations and therefore induces a
subset
Θ(P) ⊆Θ
through an interpretation map
ΦP : Impl(P) −→Θ,
where Impl(P) denotes the class of concrete instantiations (architectures together with training and
evaluation specifications) advocated by P. Importantly, ΦP is not assumed canonical: different
implementations advocated by the same program may induce different θ (e.g., via different memory
operators, evaluators, or tool interfaces), hence different admissibility properties. The map iden-
tifies representational operators, update rules, memory mechanisms, and evaluative structures as
components of
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M).
Inclusion as a conditional structural statement.
We do not assert that a program P solves
SMGI. Instead, inclusion is formulated as a condition of possibility at the level of realizations:
Graph(SemP) ∩SMGI ̸= ∅,
33


--- Page 34 ---
that is, there exist structurally admissible realizations (θ, Tθ) compatible with the SMGI obligations.
Equivalently, we make explicit the additional obligations under which a particular θ ∈Θ(P) together
with some induced Tθ ∈Sem(θ) ⊆D would qualify as an SMGI instance.
A diagnostic structural map.
Table 3 summarizes which SMGI requirements are explicitly
made formal within major AGI program classes. Marked entries indicate that a requirement is
structurally made explicit in the program’s formal narrative or mechanism class (including, for (ii),
stability properties stated at the level of induced learning dynamics). This does not constitute a
claim of completeness or satisfaction.
The map should be interpreted conservatively. Different programs make different structural
dimensions explicit: some emphasize latent predictive dynamics, others abstraction and causal
structure, others self-modification or value uncertainty. SMGI functions here as a unifying structural
criterion that makes explicit which additional obligations would be required for these mechanisms
to coexist within a single persistent learning system.
Formal interpretation of table annotations.
For a program P and requirement k ∈{(i), (ii), (iii), (iv)},
we define the explicitness predicate
ExpP(k)
to mean that requirement k is represented as an explicit formal object, constraint, admissibility
condition, or structural invariant within the program’s specification.
The symbols in Table 3 are interpreted as:
◦⇐⇒ExpP(k),
× ⇐⇒¬ExpP(k).
No global satisfaction claim is implied by this notation. The predicate concerns structural
explicitness only.
7.7
Structural Embeddings of Major AGI Paradigms
We now refine the analysis via structured embeddings.
7.7.1
A. Predictive and Representational Paradigms
World Models and Latent Predictive Dynamics.
World-model agents [Ha and Schmidhuber,
2018, Hafner et al., 2023, LeCun, 2022] factor learning into representation, latent transition, and
policy. Under ΦWM, r is instantiated as an encoder, M contains predictive latent state, Π includes
transition and policy updates.
Lemma 7.1 (Conditional SMGI embedding of latent world-model agents). Let θ ∈Θ(WM) be
induced by a world-model program. If admissible task transformations preserve a nonempty invariant
manifold S∗, the induced dynamics is stable on S∗, capacity control holds for the hypothesis/update
classes, and regime changes preserve an invariant evaluative core, then (θ, Tθ) satisfies SMGI
structural obligations.
Proof (idea). Closure corresponds to invariance of S∗; stability to bounded evolution under Tθ;
capacity control to uniform generalization bounds; evaluative invariance to persistence of protected
constraint subspaces. A complete proof depends on the chosen stability and capacity formalism
(e.g., PAC-Bayes + stability).
34


--- Page 35 ---
Theorem 7.2 (From witnesses to obligations (world-model instantiation)). Assume a world-model
induced θ ∈Θ(WM) admits (i) a typed transformation class T preserving a nonempty invariant
manifold S∗, (ii) a concrete stability witness on S∗for the induced dynamics Tθ, (iii) a concrete
capacity witness for the joint hypothesis/update class as defined below, and (iv) an invariant evaluative
core Φ preserved under regime switching. Then the four obligations (closure, stability, capacity,
evaluative invariance) hold for (θ, Tθ) in the minimal checkable sense of Definition 4.5.
Proof. (i) gives closure by invariance of S∗under T ; (ii) is stability by definition of the witness on
S∗; (iii) is capacity by the declared bound on the complexity functional; (iv) is evaluative invariance
by preservation of Φ.
Making the assumptions checkable (capacity and stability).
To avoid implicit assumptions,
we instantiate the two nontrivial hypotheses in Lemma 7.1 as explicit admissibility conditions.
Capacity control. Let H(θ) denote the joint hypothesis class encoding the encoder, latent
transition, and policy components induced by θ. We say that capacity control holds if there exists a
complexity functional C : H(θ) →R+ and a bound B < ∞such that all admissible updates preserve
C(h) ≤B, and the chosen generalization framework yields a uniform bound for the predictive/control
losses along admissible trajectories (e.g., VC/SRM-style control [Vapnik, 1998a] or PAC-Bayes
control [McAllester, 1999b, Catoni, 2007]).
Stability on the invariant manifold.
Let Tθ be the induced learning-and-control dynamics
(possibly stochastic) on the state space Sθ, and let S∗⊆Sθ be the invariant manifold postulated in
Lemma 7.1. We say that stability holds on S∗if there exists a metric (or Lyapunov-like) witness
V : S∗→R+ and constants (λ, C) such that trajectories starting in S∗remain bounded and do not
amplify perturbations under the admissible update operator (e.g., uniform stability viewpoints for
learning dynamics [Bousquet and Elisseeff, 2002, Hardt et al., 2016]). We emphasize that this is a
property of (θ, Tθ) (not θ alone), consistent with the SMGI layer separation.
Predictive Representation Programs (JEPA).
JEPA-style approaches emphasize invariances
in latent space. They contribute strongly to r and partial M structure. SMGI inclusion requires
that learned invariances align with admissible transformation classes T .
Proposition 7.3 (Transformation-aligned invariance as a closure witness). Let T be the admissible
class of task/environment transformations acting on E, and let r be the JEPA-induced representation
component. Assume that there exists a mapping Γ : T →End(R) such that for all τ ∈T and all
admissible observations x,
r(τ · x) = Γ(τ)
␀r(x)
,
with Γ(τ) an automorphism (equivariance) or the identity (invariance) on the representation space.
Then the representation layer r is compatible with SMGI closure requirement (i) under the action of
T (at the structural level), and any remaining failure of SMGI must arise from dynamical stability
(ii), capacity control (iii), or evaluative invariance (iv).
This formulation connects invariance-first programs to standard group/equivariance constraints
when T admits a group-like structure [Cohen and Welling, 2016], but does not assume that such
structure holds in general.
Abstraction and System-2 Constraints.
Programs targeting abstraction and deliberation
[Bengio, 2017, 2019] can be embedded as structural constraints on (r, M). They primarily affect
capacity control and evaluative invariance, while global stability remains a property of the induced
dynamics.
35


--- Page 36 ---
Memory stratification as the missing explicit layer.
A recurrent gap in abstraction/System-2
proposals is that the abstraction layer is specified at the level of r (or at the interface of r and E),
while the persistence of abstractions across time, regimes, and tasks is a property of the memory
operator M and its interaction with the update operator Π. In SMGI terms, this suggests making
explicit a stratified memory decomposition
M = Mepis ⊕Msem ⊕Mproc,
together with admissibility constraints limiting cross-stratum interference under updates (stability
(ii)) and ensuring that abstraction-relevant information is retained under task transformations
(closure (i)). This perspective aligns naturally with continual-learning formalisms where stability
and retention are stated explicitly (e.g., consolidation/regularization and replay-style mechanisms)
[Kirkpatrick et al., 2017, Parisi et al., 2019].
Summary (SMGI positioning).
Predictive and representational paradigms provide strong
structural leverage on closure (i) and capacity control (iii) when the representation map r and
hypothesis/update classes are explicitly typed and complexity-controlled. However, stability (ii)
and evaluative invariance (iv) remain conditional properties of the induced dynamics (θ, Tθ) and
its interaction with memory M and evaluation family L (or L): they typically require additional
admissibility constraints (e.g., stability witnesses on invariant sets, and explicitly protected evaluative
subspaces) beyond what is guaranteed by representation learning alone.
Example 1 (Two-regime tool-use with certified evaluator update). Consider an agent equipped with
a tool interface (e.g., retrieval or code execution) operating under two regimes ρ ∈R that index
sub-families Eρ(θ) ⊆E(θ): ρ0 (benign) and ρ1 (adversarial / prompt-injection). The shift ρ0 →ρ1
is formalized as an admissible task transformation τ ∈T acting on the environment family, i.e.,
τ : Eρ0(θ) →Eρ1(θ).
Let the evaluator be regime-aware,
ℓ(π; ρ) = E[Ltask(π) | ρ] + λ(ρ) E[Lsafety(π) | ρ] ,
with λ(ρ1) > λ(ρ0). An invariant evaluative core can be expressed as a protected constraint (or
testable axiom)
Φ :
E[Lsafety(π) | ρ1] ≤ε.
When applying τ (i.e., under ρ0 →ρ1), an evaluator update ℓ→ℓ′ is admissible only if it preserves
Φ and passes a declared test class (e.g., an adversarial prompt/tool-misuse suite) witnessing regime-
robustness. This illustrates evaluative invariance as certified regime adaptation rather than a fixed
external norm.
Bridge.
Example 1 concretizes why stability and evaluative invariance are not guaranteed by
representation learning alone: they require explicit witnesses (stability) and protected invariants/tests
(evaluation) that remain meaningful under typed interface transformations τ ∈T .
7.7.2
B. Meta-Level and Self-Referential Systems
Gödel Machines and Proof-Based Self-Modification.
Gödel-machine architectures [Schmid-
huber, 2006, 2010] formalize self-improvement via proof search: the system may rewrite its own code
if it proves that the rewrite increases expected utility according to a formally specified objective.
Within the SMGI meta-model, a Gödel-machine program P induces structural components as
follows:
36


--- Page 37 ---
• Π includes proof-triggered self-modification operators;
• M contains the formal theory, proof traces, and utility specification;
• E encodes the utility functional used in correctness proofs;
• r and H encode the program state and hypothesis space subject to rewrite.
Self-modification therefore acts directly on the structural level θ through admissible rewrite
operators, making Gödel machines natural candidates for modeling meta-level dynamics.
Proposition 7.4 (Structural admissibility of proof-based self-modification). Let θ ∈Θ(P) be
induced by a Gödel-machine program. If:
1. the proof system remains sound under admissible task transformations τ ∈T ;
2. self-rewrite operators preserve well-typedness of (r, H, Π, M, E);
3. complexity of admissible hypotheses remains bounded under rewrite;
then self-modification can be embedded as an admissible update operator in Π, and the induced
dynamics Tθ remains structurally well-defined.
Importantly, proof-based self-modification alone does not ensure stability (ii) or evaluative invariance
(iv): these remain dynamical and structural obligations that must be explicitly constrained beyond
logical soundness.
Inductive Logic Programming and Relational Hypothesis Update.
Inductive Logic
Programming (ILP) investigates the induction of first-order clausal theories from examples and
background knowledge, using logic programs as a universal representation for data, hypotheses, and
prior structure [Muggleton, 1991a]. This provides an explicit semantics for learning and reasoning
within a single formal object.
Under an ILP interpretation map ΦILP, a program class P induces a structural meta-model
θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) where:
• r encodes relational/symbolic representations (facts, predicates, typed signatures);
• H is a hypothesis space of logic programs (e.g., clausal theories with mode/type constraints);
• Π is an explicit induction operator (search, refinement, compression, or constraint-driven
update);
• M contains background knowledge, derivations, and (optionally) learned theory traces;
• ℓencodes the inductive criterion (coverage, compression/MDL, predictive loss, or hybrid);
• ℓ(or, in the multi-regime case, L) specifies evaluators in terms of entailment-based accuracy
and admissibility constraints.
Proposition 7.5 (Canonical structural embedding of ILP programs). Assume an ILP program class
specifies (i) a hypothesis language H of first-order theories, (ii) an explicit background knowledge
base B, and (iii) an induction/update operator Π producing theories consistent with B and data.
Then there exists θ ∈Θ(P) such that r and H encode the relational language, M contains B and
induced traces, and Π induces an admissible learning dynamics Tθ whose realizations are restricted
by the stated entailment and admissibility conditions.
37


--- Page 38 ---
ILP frameworks typically make explicit (i) closure constraints at the representational level (via
a stable symbolic language and admissibility constraints on refinements), and can make explicit
(iii) capacity control when the hypothesis language is complexity-bounded (e.g., by MDL-style or
mode/type restrictions). However, (ii) stability and (iv) evaluative invariance remain properties of
(θ, Tθ) and must be stated as explicit obligations when required.
Probabilistic ILP and Statistical Relational Learning.
Probabilistic inductive logic program-
ming extends ILP by integrating probabilistic semantics with first-order representations, yielding a
principled axis for uncertainty-aware structural learning [Raedt and Kersting, 2004, Getoor and
Taskar, 2007]. This family includes formalisms such as weighted logic templates (e.g., Markov logic
networks) [Richardson and Domingos, 2006], and probabilistic logic programming approaches.
Within SMGI, probabilistic ILP strengthens the typing of E and ℓby making uncertainty explicit,
but it does not by itself guarantee (ii) dynamical stability or (iv) invariance of evaluators under
regime changes unless these are encoded as admissibility constraints.
Algorithmic Probability, Universal Induction, and Idealized Agent Semantics.
Algo-
rithmic probability provides one of the deepest formal foundations of induction. Solomonoff’s theory
of universal induction [Solomonoff, 1964] defines a universal mixture over computable hypotheses,
weighted by description length relative to a universal reference machine. Up to an additive constant
depending on the chosen universal machine, the induced complexity measure is invariant. Compre-
hensive treatments in algorithmic information theory [Li and Vitányi, 2008] and universal prediction
[Hutter, 2005] clarify its structural implications.
Definition 7.6 (Program-prior structural bias). Let Hcomp denote a class of computable predictors
induced by programs on a fixed universal Turing machine. A structural meta-model θ is said to
admit a Solomonoff bias if H ⊆Hcomp and the loss ℓincorporates description-length–weighted
penalization consistent with a universal semimeasure.
Proposition 7.7 (Capacity control via universal priors). If θ admits a Solomonoff bias, then
requirement (iii) of SMGI (capacity control) is explicitly instantiated as a structural prior over H,
enforcing complexity-weighted hypothesis selection.
However, universal induction alone does not guarantee:
• (ii) dynamical stability of (θ, Tθ),
• (iv) invariance of evaluators L under admissible task transformations.
These remain obligations on update operators Π and memory evolution M.
Hutter’s AIXI formalism [Hutter, 2005] extends universal induction to sequential decision-making,
defining an idealized Bayes-optimal agent over computable environments. Within SMGI, AIXI can
be interpreted as a semantics-level restriction on admissible realizations of (θ, Tθ), rather than as a
structural guarantee.
Schmidhuber’s work on universal search and Gödel machines [Schmidhuber, 2002, 2006] provides
a constructive counterpart to these idealized mixtures, connecting algorithmic probability to
computable self-improving systems.
However, even in these constructive formulations, claims
of asymptotic optimality do not by themselves ensure stability (ii) or evaluator invariance (iv);
additional admissibility constraints are required to prevent uncontrolled structural drift under
self-modification.
38


--- Page 39 ---
Verification relative to SMGI obligations.
• (i) Closure: classes of computable environments are closed under computable transformations
but not necessarily under arbitrary regime shifts that alter evaluator structure or admissibility
constraints.
• (ii) Stability: incomputable universal mixtures do not imply bounded learning dynamics.
• (iii) Capacity: explicitly controlled via description-length prior.
• (iv) Evaluative invariance: must be separately encoded.
Modern consolidation and explicit SMGI obligations.
For a modern consolidation of uni-
versal induction and its philosophical/technical status (including the role of universal semimeasures
and invariance caveats), see contemporary treatises and monographs [Li and Vitányi, 2008, Hutter,
2005]. This literature makes explicit a key SMGI distinction: universal priors provide a principled
structural bias (supporting (iii)), while agent-level optimality statements are semantics-level and
often incomputable, hence require realizability constraints at the level of (θ, Tθ).
Lemma 7.8 (Realizability obligations for universal-mixture approximations). Any realizable approx-
imation of universal-mixture prediction or AIXI-style decision rules induces additional admissibility
constraints Ωon (Π, M, E). In SMGI terms, (ii) stability and (iv) evaluative invariance are satisfied
only if Ωexplicitly controls (a) update-induced drift in Π, (b) memory interference across regimes in
M, and (c) evaluator preservation in L under admissible transformations.
This positions the algorithmic-probability axis as complementary to SMGI: it strengthens (iii) at
the structural level, while SMGI supplies the missing cross-regime admissibility layer required for
(ii) and (iv).
Thus, algorithmic-probability–based frameworks contribute a principled structural prior, but
intersect with SMGI only under additional admissibility constraints.
Meta-Learning as Structured Higher-Order Adaptation.
Meta-learning (“learning to learn”)
elevates the update operator itself to a first-class object. Rather than merely optimizing parameters
within a fixed hypothesis class, a meta-learning system modifies the way hypotheses are updated
across tasks.
Within SMGI, this corresponds to enriching the update component Π and its induced dynamics
Tθ, while often introducing additional memory strata in M. We distinguish four structurally distinct
forms of meta-adaptation.
(1) Parameter-Level Meta-Updates (Gradient-Based Meta-Learning).
Gradient-
based meta-learning (GBML) frameworks such as MAML [Finn et al., 2017] learn an initialization θ
such that task-specific parameters ϕi are obtained by
ϕi = θ −α∇θLTi(θ).
First-order approximations (e.g., Reptile) simplify this bi-level optimization [Nichol and Schulman,
2018].
Definition 7.9 (Two-Level Update Structure). Let Πin be a task-level update operator and Πout a
meta-update acting across tasks. We say that θ admits explicit meta-structure if Π = (Πin, Πout)
induces a coupled dynamics Tθ on task parameters and meta-parameters.
39


--- Page 40 ---
Recent work extends GBML to long-context state-space architectures, demonstrating that
continual or rapid adaptation can be implemented within selective state-space models [Zhao and
Gong, 2024]. This strengthens the structural role of Π but leaves stability (ii) dependent on explicit
regularity constraints.
(2) Metric and Embedding-Based Meta-Adaptation.
Metric-based approaches (Match-
ing Networks, Prototypical Networks, Relation Networks) [Vinyals et al., 2016, Snell et al., 2017,
Sung et al., 2018] replace gradient adaptation with geometric comparison in a learned embedding
space.
Under Φmetric:
• r defines an embedding map into a metric space;
• M stores support examples or prototypes;
• Πin reduces to prototype computation or similarity evaluation.
Capacity control (iii) becomes a geometric constraint on the embedding family, while stability
(ii) depends on invariance of the metric representation under admissible task transformations.
(3) Memory-Based and Black-Box Meta-Learning.
Memory-augmented meta-learning
systems encode fast adaptation within internal or external memory states [Santoro et al., 2016,
Mishra et al., 2017]. Rather than updating parameters, adaptation is implemented via evolution of
hidden state.
More recent architectures introduce persistent neural memory modules trained to update during
inference (e.g., Titans-style architectures) [Behrouz et al., 2025].
Within SMGI:
• M is stratified into persistent memory and fast-adaptation memory;
• Π includes admissible memory-update operators;
• stability (ii) becomes a dynamical constraint on interference and drift in memory state.
(4) Inference-Time Meta-Learning (ICL and Test-Time Adaptation).
In-context
learning (ICL) exhibits adaptation without parameter updates [Brown et al., 2020]. MetaICL
explicitly optimizes for this capability by training on distributions of task episodes [Min et al., 2022].
Theoretical analyses demonstrate that Transformers trained for ICL can implement multi-step
gradient descent internally [von Oswald et al., 2023], establishing that forward-pass attention
dynamics may instantiate an implicit update operator.
Test-Time Training (TTT) frameworks explicitly activate gradient-based parameter updates
during inference, optimizing auxiliary self-supervised objectives on incoming data [Sun et al., 2024].
Unlike pure in-context learning, TTT modifies persistent parameters, thus directly affecting Πparam
at deployment.
In SMGI terms, TTT makes explicit that inference-time adaptation is not merely contextual,
but alters the structural state of θ. Stability (ii) therefore requires bounded test-time drift to avoid
regime-dependent structural collapse.
In SMGI terms:
• M contains a transient context layer Mctx;
40


--- Page 41 ---
• Πin may be implemented by forward-pass computation or local test-time gradient descent;
• stability (ii) requires bounded interaction between context memory and persistent parameters;
• evaluative invariance (iv) requires preservation of L under extended reasoning loops.
PAC-Bayesian Meta-Generalization.
Recent work formalizes meta-learning within PAC-
Bayesian frameworks, providing explicit generalization guarantees across task distributions. Let
Q denote a posterior over meta-parameters and P a prior. Meta-generalization bounds take the
schematic form
Lmeta(Q) ≤ˆLmeta(Q) +
s
KL(Q∥P) + ln(1/δ)
2ntasks
,
where ntasks is the number of observed tasks.
Within SMGI, such bounds strengthen requirement (iii) by explicitly controlling inter-task
capacity, but do not by themselves guarantee dynamical stability (ii) or evaluator invariance (iv).
Structural Verification Relative to SMGI.
Across all four families:
• (i) Closure requires explicit typing of task families and update admissibility.
• (ii) Stability is a property of (θ, Tθ), not of θ alone.
• (iii) Capacity control must be enforced either via hypothesis complexity, meta-regularization,
or geometric constraints.
• (iv) Evaluative invariance must remain stable across task and context regimes.
Thus, meta-learning enriches Π and M by introducing higher-order update structures and
memory stratification, but intersects with SMGI only when its dynamical behavior is constrained
by explicit admissibility conditions.
B5. A Transversal Comparative Theorem (Meta-Update, Memory, and Admissibility).
To compare heterogeneous meta-learning and self-referential paradigms without reductionism, we
formulate a transversal result at the level of admissible embeddings into SMGI.
Definition 7.10 (Meta-adaptation mode as an update factorization). Fix θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M)
and its induced dynamics Tθ. We say that a program class P admits a meta-adaptation factorization
if its effective adaptation can be typed as one (or a composition) of:
Π ≡Πparam ◦Πmem ◦Πctx ◦Πinf,
where: Πparam updates persistent parameters (training-time or test-time training), Πmem updates
persistent/episodic memory strata, Πctx updates transient context memory Mctx, and Πinf denotes
inference-time internal computation implementing an implicit update (e.g., multi-step internal
optimization). This factorization is purely typological: it does not assert that any component is
stable or optimal.
Theorem 7.11 (Transversal SMGI obligations for meta-level systems). Let P be any program
class whose adaptation mechanism admits a meta-adaptation factorization, and let Θ(P) ⊆Θ be
its induced structural subclass with semantics SemP. Assume there exists θ ∈Θ(P) such that each
41


--- Page 42 ---
update component in the factorization is well-typed and admissible (domain/codomain consistent
with (r, H, M)).
Then the following are necessary structural/dynamical obligations for
Graph(SemP) ∩SMGI ̸= ∅:
1. Closure typing (i). The admissible transformation class T must preserve the typing of the
factorization: under τ ∈T , each component Πparam, Πmem, Πctx, Πinf remains a well-defined
operator on its intended state space (parameters, memory strata, context window, inference
trace).
2. Dynamical stability (ii) is factor-sensitive. Stability of the overall evolution is a property
of (θ, Tθ) and requires explicit constraints on each active factor: bounded parameter drift for
Πparam, bounded interference for Πmem, bounded context amplification for Πctx, and bounded
inference recursion depth/energy for Πinf.
3. Capacity control (iii) decomposes across factors. A sufficient capacity statement must
control not only H, but also the effective complexity induced by meta-updates: model class
complexity (parameters), memory complexity (storage/retrieval), context complexity (prompt
bandwidth), and inference-computation complexity (implicit optimization steps).
4. Evaluative invariance (iv) requires a protected core. If E is allowed to change under
regime shifts or self-modification, then evaluative invariance fails unless there exists an explicitly
protected evaluative core E⋆⊆E invariant under admissible updates and transformations.
Conversely, if there exists θ ∈Θ(P) and an admissibility bundle Ωthat explicitly enforces (i)–(iv)
at the factor level, then
(θ, Tθ) ∈SMGI
⇒
Graph(SemP) ∩SMGI ̸= ∅.
Corollary 7.12 (Why no paradigm “satisfies SMGI” by default). For each of the major families
discussed above (GBML, metric/meta-embedding, memory-based meta-learning, ICL/test-time adap-
tation, self-referential update systems), SMGI membership is not a built-in property of the narrative
mechanism. It only arises for those realizations whose admissibility bundle Ωstabilizes the induced
dynamics and preserves an evaluative core.
Remark 7.13 (Direct link to the SMGI meta-structure). The theorem makes explicit the role of SMGI
as a coordination layer: it does not replace any paradigm’s internal learning principle, but imposes
cross-component admissibility constraints ensuring that meta-updates, memory stratification, and
evaluators can coexist in a single persistent system.
7.7.3
C. Structural Constraints on Environment and Interaction
Causal and Intervention Models.
Structural causal models [Pearl, 2009, Spirtes et al., 2000]
constrain admissible transformations and representation. They provide explicit transformation
semantics but do not by themselves define persistent multi-component learning dynamics.
Invariant causal prediction [Peters et al., 2016] formalizes robustness of structural relations
across environments, providing a statistical interpretation of admissible transformation invariance.
Definition 7.14 (Causal structural embedding). Let P be a program class based on structural
causal models. A structural embedding into Θ consists of:
42


--- Page 43 ---
• r encoding structural equations and intervention semantics;
• H restricted to hypothesis classes compatible with causal graph constraints;
• L including counterfactual or interventional evaluation functionals;
• Π preserving acyclicity or admissible structural updates.
Definition 7.15 (Intervention-typed admissible transformations). Let T denote the admissible
transformation class in SMGI. In causal frameworks, we call τ ∈T intervention-typed if it admits
an explicit semantics as an intervention (e.g., do-operator or equivalent structural manipulation) on
a structural causal model, so that causal queries and invariance claims remain well-typed under τ.
Proposition 7.16 (Causal formulation of SMGI-(i) closure). If admissible regime shifts are restricted
to intervention-typed transformations, then requirement (i) (closure) can be stated as preservation
of the causal semantics under T (invariance of the relevant structural equations and interventional
predictions under admissible transformations).
Lemma 7.17 (Factorization preservation under admissible transformations). Let G denote the
directed acyclic graph associated with a structural causal model embedded in r.
If admissible
transformations τ ∈T preserve the Markov factorization induced by G, then closure (i) reduces to
invariance of the induced conditional independence structure.
Verification relative to SMGI obligations (causal axis).
• (i) Closure: enforced by restricting T to intervention-typed transformations.
• (ii) Stability: requires bounds on update-induced drift when causal models are learned online.
• (iii) Capacity: depends on complexity control of the causal hypothesis class (graphs/structural
equations).
• (iv) Evaluative invariance: requires separating causal goals/constraints in L from regime-
contingent proxies.
Epistemic and Theory-of-Mind Foundations.
McCarthy’s epistemological program [Mc-
Carthy, 1959, McCarthy and Hayes, 1969, McCarthy, 1979] treats belief and context as first-class
objects. Epistemic logic [Fagin et al., 1995] and BDI architectures [Wooldridge, 2000] formalize
multi-agent reasoning.
Under ΦToM, r constructs belief states, M stores epistemic traces, E becomes multi-agent. SMGI
obligations require bounded recursive belief depth, stable belief-update operators, and closure under
social configuration transformations.
Proposition 7.18 (Bounded epistemic recursion as stability condition). Let Bi denote the belief
operator for agent i. If recursive belief nesting depth is uniformly bounded, and belief-update operators
are contraction mappings in a suitable metric space, then stability (ii) can be formulated as bounded
epistemic divergence.
43


--- Page 44 ---
Active Inference and Free-Energy Minimization.
Active inference frameworks [Friston, 2010,
Friston et al., 2017] model agents as minimizing variational free energy under a generative model.
Under ΦAI:
• r encodes a generative world model;
• M stores posterior beliefs;
• Π corresponds to variational updates;
• E reflects expected free-energy minimization.
Verification relative to SMGI obligations.
• (i) Closure: holds only if structural equations or belief-update rules remain invariant under
admissible regime transformations.
• (ii) Stability: depends on bounded belief revision or variational update convergence.
• (iii) Capacity: controlled via structural graph complexity or generative model class.
• (iv) Evaluative invariance: requires preservation of interventional or epistemic semantics across
regimes.
Causal and epistemic paradigms primarily constrain admissible transformation classes T and
refine the internal structure of r through structural equations or belief operators. However, they
do not by themselves impose cross-regime dynamical admissibility on (θ, Tθ).
SMGI extends
these paradigms by coupling representational invariance with explicit stability (ii) and evaluator
preservation (iv), thereby elevating transformation semantics into a persistent meta-model constraint.
7.7.4
D. Cognitive and Hybrid Architectures
These architectures are historically important because they make memory stratification and control
explicit design constraints rather than emergent side effects: ACT-R enforces separations between
declarative/procedural stores and buffers [Anderson, 1996, Anderson et al., 2004], SOAR formalizes
semantic/episodic memory with explicit learning mechanisms [Laird, 2012], LIDA operationalizes
global-workspace-driven cognitive cycles with distinct memory systems [Franklin et al., 2011], and
NARS treats reasoning under insufficient knowledge/resources with explicit memory and control
constructs [Wang, 2006].
Under ΦHYB, these systems induce structured meta-models in which:
• r includes typed symbolic or sub-symbolic representational layers;
• M is stratified into working memory, episodic memory, procedural memory, and long-term
knowledge stores;
• Π can encode inductive biases and admissibility constraints that are realized in Tθ via rule-
based, production-based, or hybrid update operators.
Definition 7.19 (Memory stratification). A structural meta-model θ = (r, H, Π, L, E, M) ad-
mits an explicit memory stratification if M is given as a typed tuple of strata (e.g., work-
ing/episodic/semantic/procedural) and Π contains explicit sub-operators governing admissible
read/write/consolidation/forgetting maps between these strata.
44


--- Page 45 ---
Lemma 7.20 (Memory stratification is a structural constraint, not an implementation detail). If
M is stratified and Π contains typed inter-stratum operators, then admissibility under regime shifts
cannot be stated solely at the level of task loss ℓ: it must constrain (a) interference across strata, (b)
consolidation/forgetting operators, and (c) evaluator access to protected memory subspaces. Hence
stability (ii) and evaluative invariance (iv) are inherently coupled to the stratification design.
Proposition 7.21 (Hybrid-architectural embedding). If a cognitive architecture specifies explicit
typing of its memory strata and admissible update operators between modules, then it induces a
subclass Θ(P) ⊆Θ in which memory stratification is structurally explicit.
Verification relative to SMGI.
• (i) Closure: depends on whether module interactions remain typed under regime shifts.
• (ii) Stability: requires bounded cross-module feedback loops.
• (iii) Capacity: depends on representational and rule-complexity bounds.
• (iv) Evaluative invariance: requires preservation of goal modules under adaptation.
Thus, cognitive architectures make memory structure explicit, but SMGI membership requires
additional admissibility constraints ensuring stable multi-module coordination.
Structural contrast with end-to-end neural systems.
Unlike end-to-end deep architectures,
where memory and control often emerge implicitly within distributed parameters, classical cognitive
architectures treat memory stratification as a first-class structural object. Within SMGI, this
difference is not merely historical: it distinguishes systems where admissibility constraints can be
stated explicitly at the level of M from those where such constraints must be reconstructed post
hoc from global dynamics.
This clarifies the structural contribution of hybrid architectures: they anticipate, at the design
level, the necessity of typed memory control that SMGI formalizes abstractly. Importantly, SMGI
does not require modular cognitive architectures; it abstracts the structural role of stratified memory
independently of whether it is implemented symbolically, neurally, or emergently.
7.7.5
E. Large-Scale Generalist and Agentic Systems
Large-scale generalist systems unify heterogeneous modalities (text, vision, action, code, simulation)
within a single parameterized model [Reed et al., 2022, Yao et al., 2023, Park et al., 2023, Bommasani
and et al., 2021, Kaplan et al., 2020]. Their defining feature is empirical interface unification: a
single set of parameters supports multiple task families through prompting, instruction tuning, or
reinforcement learning.
Under ΦGEN, such programs induce structural meta-models θ ∈Θ(P) characterized by:
• broad hypothesis classes H defined by large neural function families;
• unified representational maps r shared across modalities;
• partially structured update mechanisms Π, typically combining pretraining, instruction tuning,
and reinforcement-based post-training (e.g., RLHF).
45


--- Page 46 ---
Definition 7.22 (Interface-level generality). A program class P exhibits interface generality
if a single parameterized model supports multiple modalities or task families through a shared
representation and inference mechanism.
Interface generality is an empirical property of realizations, not a structural constraint on
admissible transformations.
Proposition 7.23 (Interface unification does not imply structural admissibility). Let P be a
large-scale generalist program class with unified parameters across tasks. Then the existence of
a single parameterized model supporting multiple modalities or tasks does not imply that (θ, Tθ)
satisfies:
• closure (i) under typed regime transformations,
• stability (ii) under admissible update dynamics,
• capacity control (iii) in a complexity-theoretic sense,
• evaluative invariance (iv) under structural perturbations,
unless these properties are explicitly formalized at the level of the structural meta-model (θ, Tθ).
In particular, large-scale generalist systems define a broad empirical realization class within Θ, but
they do not, by construction, restrict Θ(P) to meta-models satisfying admissibility constraints.
Thus, generality at the realization level does not induce structural restriction at the meta-model
level.
Verification relative to SMGI obligations.
In SMGI, verification is not a standalone guarantee:
it is defined relative to a set of explicit obligations that make “correctness” well-posed under structural
evolution. Concretely, a verifier is meaningful only with respect to (i) a typed transformation class
(closure), (ii) certified drift control along admissible updates (stability), (iii) structural capacity
control under the induced dynamics (capacity), and (iv) protected evaluators and norms that remain
invariant across regime shifts (evaluative invariance).1 These obligations jointly specify what may
change, what must remain invariant, and what must be checked at every step; the items below
instantiate each obligation and explain why purely empirical robustness or post-training objectives
do not suffice without typed transformations and certificate-gated evolution.
• (i) Closure / Typed Transformations (modern formalisms as instances): A large
body of work formalizes generalization under fixed learning interfaces, for instance via domain
adaptation and distribution shift theory Ben-David et al. [2010], invariance-based formulations
Arjovsky et al. [2019], and test-time adaptation under shift Liang et al. [2020].
These
approaches typically treat changes as occurring within a predefined input–output interface and
hypothesis class, and do not by themselves endow transformations with a typed semantics.
Within SMGI, closure requires an explicit transformation class T equipped with semantics:
each g ∈T specifies what structural component is transformed (e.g., interface r, evaluator ℓ
(or regime family L), memory policy M) together with admissibility and composition rules.
Prompt variation or modality switching, for example, is merely an interface perturbation
unless represented as a typed transformation with pre/post-conditions. This typing makes
1We use θ ≜(θcore, K(θcore)) and write Tθ for the induced semantics.
46


--- Page 47 ---
“coverage” and reachability claims well-posed: SMGI does not assume closure; it defines the
allowable transformation space and restricts certified evolution to updates for which certificates
can be constructed.
• (ii) Stability / Certified Drift Control (modern stability views as instances):
Stability is a classical route to generalization guarantees, ranging from algorithmic stability
Bousquet and Elisseeff [2002] to stability of stochastic gradient methods Hardt et al. [2016]
and robustness under distributional perturbations.
In modern large-scale systems, empirical robustness and scaling can correlate with improved
average performance Kaplan et al. [2020], but such empirical trends do not constitute a
dynamical stability guarantee for an evolving agent.
In SMGI, stability is defined as a property of certified evolution: updates must satisfy explicit
bounds on (a) update-induced drift (structural cost Ωand invariant drift dI), (b) memory
interference across regimes, and (c) persistence of designated invariant substructures. The key
point is that stability is not inferred from scaling; it is enforced by restricting evolution to
admissible updates Uθcore and by attaching monitorable stability obligations to certificates.
This makes stability checkable under regime switching: even when the task interface changes,
the verifier and invariants constrain the induced semantics Tθ so that uncontrolled drift is
ruled out by construction.
• (iii) Capacity and Implicit Regularization (modern phenomena as instances):
Classical scaling laws describe empirical performance regularities as model size increases
Kaplan et al. [2020]. Modern theory and mechanistic analyses of overparameterized systems—
including NTK-like limits Jacot et al. [2018], implicit regularization along optimization
trajectories Arora et al. [2019], and delayed generalization phenomena such as grokking Power
et al. [2022], Wang et al. [2024]—show that generalization is often governed by implicit
constraints induced by the update operator (e.g., SGD/Adam) rather than by static VC-style
bounds.
Within SMGI, these phenomena are interpreted as trajectory-dependent capacity control of the
induced semantics Tθ: for example, grokking corresponds to a delayed entry of the learning
trajectory into an admissible invariant set S∗
θ. Crucially, most existing analyses explain implicit
regularization under a stationary evaluative regime (effectively K = 1) and a fixed interface.
SMGI generalizes beyond this setting by lifting capacity control to the structural level: the
admissible update set Uθcore constrains not only parameters but also permissible changes to the
learning interface, memory policies, and the norm/verification layer. This enables statements
about generalization and stability under regime shifts and interface transformations, where
purely parameter-space implicit regularization is insufficient.
• (iv) Evaluative Invariance / Protected Evaluators (post-training as an instance):
Post-training and preference optimization (e.g., RLHF and related alignment pipelines)
introduce explicit evaluative components and reward models that shape behavior Christiano
et al. [2017], Ouyang et al. [2022]. However, such procedures do not guarantee that evaluators
remain invariant under regime shifts, tool changes, or self-modification; indeed, “moving-target”
evaluation is a central difficulty in agentic settings.
SMGI treats evaluators and normative constraints as protected objects: evaluative components
L and norms in the control layer K(θcore) must be preserved (or updated only through certified
meta-updates) to prevent evaluation drift. Thus, evaluative invariance is not assumed; it is
47


--- Page 48 ---
imposed as an explicit obligation enforced by certificates and invariant constraints. This is
precisely what enables multi-regime control: regime switching may alter tasks and interfaces,
but the integrity of evaluators and constraints remains stable unless an explicitly certified
transformation updates them.
7.8
Philosophical and Conceptual Constraints
Philosophical analysis is not invoked here as external commentary, but as a constraint-generator on
admissible semantics and evaluators. The central role of this subsection is to justify why SMGI
must keep capability, semantic commitment, and evaluation as formally distinct layers, and why
invariance claims cannot be inferred from scale or representational power alone.
Capability–evaluation separability.
The orthogonality thesis [Bostrom, 2014] motivates treat-
ing evaluative structure as independent from capability scaling: a system may become more
competent while its evaluative behavior drifts under self-modification. Within SMGI, this motivates
a structural separation between representational/hypothesis components (r, H) and the evaluative
layer (active evaluator ℓt and, in the multi-regime case, the evaluator family L = {ℓk}K
k=1).
Formally, the orthogonality thesis can be re-expressed as the non-implication:
Cap(r,H) ̸⇒Inv(L),
where Cap(r,H) denotes expansion in representational or hypothesis capacity, and Inv(L) denotes
invariance (or protected-core invariance) of the evaluative structure under admissible transformations.
This structural restatement clarifies that scaling or competence gains do not constrain the dynamical
evolution of evaluation unless protection constraints are explicitly imposed.
Definition 7.24 (Evaluative core and protection requirement). An evaluative core is a designated
substructure Lcore ⊆L together with a protection predicate Prot(Lcore) stating that admissible
updates and regime shifts preserve the semantics of Lcore (up to an explicitly specified equivalence).
Proposition 7.25 (Capability increase does not entail evaluative invariance). Even if scaling laws
yield systematic performance gains for a realization class of agents, this does not imply evaluative
invariance (iv): unless Prot(Lcore) is imposed as an explicit admissibility constraint, nothing prevents
evaluator drift (i.e., drift of ℓt or of the induced regime structure) under learning, fine-tuning, or
self-modification.
Intentionality and semantic admissibility.
Arguments about intentionality [Searle, 1980]
highlight the distinction between syntactic competence and semantic grounding. Within SMGI, this
distinction is operationalized as a boundary condition on admissible semantics: we do not infer
semantic commitment from behavioral competence alone. Rather, semantic claims are treated as
restrictions on SemP (the realization semantics), and on which transformations count as meaning-
preserving in T .
Definition 7.26 (Semantic admissibility boundary). A program class P admits a semantic admis-
sibility boundary if the realization semantics SemP restricts (θ, Tθ) to those realizations for which
meaning-preserving transformations are explicitly typed in T , and evaluator access to semantic
content is explicitly specified.
48


--- Page 49 ---
Induction, underdetermination, and regime shifts.
Classic problems of induction and theory
choice (Humean underdetermination and the non-uniqueness of inductive bias [Hume, 1739], the role
of falsifiability constraints [Popper, 1959], and the dependence of explanation on representational
schemes [Carnap, 1950]) justify why closure (i) must be typed and why stability (ii) cannot be
assumed. In particular, multiple hypothesis/update pairs can fit the same experience, so regime
shifts expose latent degrees of freedom in Π and M that can produce uncontrolled drift.
Lemma 7.27 (Underdetermination induces evaluator-risk under self-modification). If multiple
realizations (θ, Tθ) are observationally adequate over a regime but differ in their induced evaluator
interaction, then a regime shift or self-modification can select a realization with the same competence
but different evaluative behavior. Hence (iv) cannot be inferred from competence or fit alone.
Value learning as an explicit evaluator-model relation.
Work on value alignment and
preference inference emphasizes that evaluators should not be treated as fixed consequences of
capability, but as objects to be inferred or stabilized [Ng and Russell, 2000, Hadfield-Menell et al.,
2016a, Russell, 2019]. Within SMGI this motivates modeling evaluators as first-class structures in
L, and treating their preservation as an explicit admissibility condition rather than an emergent
property of scale.
Implication for SMGI.
These philosophical constraints jointly justify the SMGI discipline: (i)
closure must be defined over typed regime transformations, (ii) stability must be attributed to (θ, Tθ)
rather than θ alone, (iii) capacity control must be explicit rather than assumed from performance,
and (iv) evaluative invariance requires protected cores and admissibility constraints.
Requisite variety and closure constraints.
Ashby’s law of requisite variety [Ashby, 1956]
states that effective control requires internal variety at least matching environmental variety. In
structural terms, closure (i) requires that the admissible hypothesis class H possess sufficient
structural diversity relative to the regime transformation class T . Without such matching variety,
stability (ii) and evaluative invariance (iv) cannot be maintained under perturbation.
Cybernetic stability principles [Wiener, 1948] further justify attributing stability (ii) to feedback-
structured dynamics (θ, Tθ) rather than static architecture. In particular, evaluator preservation
requires closed-loop constraints ensuring bounded propagation of perturbations across representa-
tional and memory components.
This perspective is aligned with Marr’s levels of analysis [Marr, 1982], which distinguish compu-
tational, algorithmic, and implementational levels. SMGI operates at an extended computational
level: it specifies structural admissibility constraints independently of particular learning algorithms
or substrates.
Accordingly, SMGI does not assume alignment between representational power and goal stability,
nor between empirical success and semantic commitment. It instead imposes a structural discipline:
evaluators must be explicitly protected, semantic admissibility must be typed, closure must match
environmental variety, and stability must be ensured by bounded feedback dynamics. Philosophical
analysis thus functions not as commentary, but as a generator of structural admissibility constraints.
7.9
Synthesis and Transition to Formal Meta-Structure
The preceding positioning analysis reveals a structural convergence across predictive modeling,
meta-learning, causal reasoning, cognitive architectures, and large-scale generalist systems. Despite
methodological diversity, these paradigms recurrently introduce:
49


--- Page 50 ---
• persistent representational strata,
• compositional or hierarchical update operators,
• explicit or implicit memory organization,
• partial invariance constraints under transformation.
Structural convergence without structural unification.
Let Pi denote each paradigm-specific
program class. Each induces a subclass Θ(Pi) ⊆Θ. While these subclasses exhibit overlapping
structural motifs, none defines a global admissibility operator:
A : Θ →{0, 1}
that simultaneously enforces closure (i), stability (ii), capacity control (iii), and evaluative
invariance (iv) within a unified persistent meta-model.
Proposition 7.28 (Paradigmatic insufficiency). For every individual paradigm Pi examined, there
exist realizations θ ∈Θ(Pi) that satisfy some but not all SMGI obligations. Hence no examined
paradigm, in isolation, defines a structurally sufficient admissibility layer.
This establishes that structural convergence does not entail structural completeness.
Systemic coherence as admissibility criterion.
Coherence theories of justification [BonJour,
1985] emphasize that adequacy is a property of systems, not isolated components. Analogously,
admissibility within SMGI is a property of the coupled tuple:
(θ, Tθ) = (r, H, Π, ℓ, E, M, Tθ),
rather than any single structural component. Admissibility therefore requires global compatibility
between representation, update, memory, and evaluator preservation.
Elevation to explicit structural object.
SMGI formalizes this missing layer by elevating
distributed requirements to a typed structural object:
θ ∈Θ
such that
(θ, Tθ) ∈A.
Here A denotes the admissibility class defined by closure, stability, capacity control, and
evaluative invariance.
This shifts the analysis from paradigm-specific heuristics to meta-level
structural constraints.
Transition.
The positioning section has therefore demonstrated:
1. empirical convergence across traditions,
2. theoretical insufficiency of isolated paradigms,
3. necessity of an explicit admissibility layer.
We now proceed to construct this layer formally. The next section introduces the meta-structure
of Θ and specifies the admissibility operator A as a mathematically explicit constraint on (θ, Tθ).
50


--- Page 51 ---
8
Deep Comparative Analysis with Frontier AI Systems (2023–
2026)
8.1
Overview
Recent advances in AI (2023–2026) have produced systems exhibiting unprecedented performance
across language, multimodality, reasoning, planning, and tool use. These include:
• GPT-4 and GPT-4o OpenAI [2023a, 2024a],
• Claude 3 family Anthropic [2024],
• Gemini 1.5 Team [2024b],
• DeepSeek-V3 Team [2024a],
• Qwen2.5-Omni Team [2024c],
• Generalist agents such as Gato Reed et al. [2022],
• Tool-augmented LLM agents Yao et al. [2023],
• World-model systems (MuZero, DreamerV3) Schrittwieser et al. [2020], Hafner et al. [2023].
While these systems significantly expand task coverage and scaling capabilities, we show that
they remain instances of structurally fixed evaluative regimes.
Our framework subsumes them as special cases. Our comparative claims below rely on the strict
inclusion result proved in Theorem 4.7 (Section 4.9).
8.2
Large Language Models (LLMs)
Modern LLMs optimize:
min
θ
Ex∼D [−log pθ(x)] .
Alignment methods (RLHF, DPO) introduce preference-based losses:
max
θ
E(x,y+,y−) log σ

β(fθ(x, y+) −fθ(x, y−))

Rafailov et al. [2023].
However:
• The representation r is fixed post-training.
• The loss ℓis externally specified.
• Regime plurality is implicit (prompt-driven), not structurally modeled.
• Memory/forgetting is architectural (context window), not evaluative.
In our notation:
K = 1,
σ trivial,
M fixed.
Thus LLMs correspond to a degenerate structural configuration:
θLLM = (r, H, Π, L, E, M0),
L = {ℓ}.
51


--- Page 52 ---
8.3
Tool-Augmented Agents
ReAct-style agents Yao et al. [2023] interleave reasoning and tool calls.
These systems expand operational capacity but retain:
• single reward/evaluation structure,
• no explicit modeling of antagonistic regimes,
• no learned regime commutation.
They correspond to:
Hagent = HLLM ◦Tools
without structural modification of evaluation layer.
8.4
World Models and Model-Based RL
MuZero Schrittwieser et al. [2020] learns:
( ˆf, ˆg, ˆr)
representing dynamics and reward.
DreamerV3 Hafner et al. [2023] optimizes:
max
π
E
"X
t
γtrt
#
.
Key property:
Reward rt defines a single evaluative regime.
No structural mechanism for switching evaluative regimes exists.
Thus:
K = 1,
σ undefined.
Our framework strictly generalizes by allowing:
{r(k)
t
}K
k=1
with context-dependent switching.
8.5
AIXI and Universal Agents
AIXI Hutter [2005] optimizes:
at = arg max
a
Eξ
" ∞
X
k=t
rk
#
where ξ is a Solomonoff mixture.
